implications: FAST CASH FLOW: Distillation/quantization scripts (distill-strategy.mjs, quantization-oracle.mjs) + scoped-key auth (mintScopedKey, keyHasScope) can be extracted, wrapped, and sold as a self-serve 'API Permission Audit' within 2-3 weeks (low lift, high margin on $25k fixed fee).

DURABLE FOUNDATION: Trace-capture (W3C Trace Context) + event-schema (W369 canonical fields) + capture-store (durable async) provide a reusable data plane for FUTURE products (e.g., Okta-for-AI-Agents style fine-grained auth; Vorlon-style multi-tenant agentic governance). Do NOT fork; modularize instead.

ATTESTATION-READY PRIMITIVES: Ed25519 keys.js + intoto-receipt.js + auditor-attestation.js + trace verification chain are already production-grade; reuse the DSSE envelope, block validation, and cross-check logic. The product's only custom work is permission-analysis logic + report formatting.

REGULATORY BRIDGE: PII redaction service (pii-redactor.js stateless HTTP) + event-level holdout_only flag + tenant receipt-secret rotation (auth.js rotateTenantReceiptSecret) already map to SOC2 Type II 'logging and monitoring' + 'encryption' controls. Audit story is 60% done; finish with API audit trail + signed attestation emission.

MULTI-TENANT ISOLATION: trace-capture.js W425 tenant_id ownership fence + capture-store.js tenant-scoped reads + auth.js scoped keys give you N-to-1 audit (one auditor signs attestations for M startups' artifacts). Revenue scaling at low marginal cost.

COLD-START ADDRESSABILITY: $25k/mo implies 8 fixed customers at full $25k or 40 at $5k. Founder's network is AI-native startups in security review. Quantization-oracle.mjs (device targeting) is a lead-gen hook ('your model can run on smaller device for $X cost; here's the audit that proves it'). Pair with scoped-key demo (show API permissions at a glance).

RISK VECTOR: Prompt injection defense is NOT in baseline kolm; if buyer's agentic product runs untrusted code (e.g., agent-blueprint.js), the attestation's 'secure' claim becomes liability. Define strict scope: 'attests API permission posture + traffic redaction; does NOT cover prompt/instruction injection'. Charge separately for that layer.

RETAINER PATH: First 8 weeks is fixed-fee + contingency (signed attestation unlocks enterprise deal = upside 20%). Retainer cycle: monthly re-attestation (incremental deltas, lower cost) + quarterly posture reassessment. Trace-capture is append-only by design; rolling audits are cheap.

INTEGRATION HOOK: Gateway-router.js (W742 dispatch) + mcp-gateway-routes.js (W921 MCP server routes) show kolm already proxies agentic calls. Product can live as a sidecar service (redactor.js model) or as a /v1/audit/* namespace in the main router, inheriting the auth stack.

brief.the_user: Seed-to-Series-B AI-native startup, concentrated at Series A (median raise ~$30-75M), 12-24 months old, 20-60 engineers, founder-led with no dedicated CPO/security FTE. They sell an agentic product (vertical agent for support/SDR/coding/doc-analysis, horizontal agent builder, or agent infra) INTO an enterprise. Economic buyer = technical founder or CTO making the readiness call; Head of Sales/Sales Ops gets pulled in once they realize security is the blocker; a secondary "Series-A diligence prep" buyer exists (founder wants to look ready for VCs). They are NOT the enterprise and NOT a security vendor. Addressable cohort: ~100-150 AI-native startups actively selling agentic products into enterprises, ~13 agentic Series-A rounds in the last 12 months; founder's warm network is exactly this group.
brief.the_pain: One specific enterprise deal worth $100K-$500K ACV is STALLED (not lost) in the buyer's security review, which has stretched from the normal 5-10 business days to 4-8 weeks because the agentic product is over-permissioned (90% of agents carry ~10x needed privileges; 45.6% share API keys), has no append-only/tamper-evident audit trail (33% have none), no SOC2 Type II (now required by ~80% of enterprise buyers), and no prompt-injection/least-privilege story. JTBD: "Get our agent through the enterprise security review and close this deal without losing the quarter." Roughly 1 in 3 deals stall this way. The cost is asymmetric and time-boxed: quarter-end revenue slips, the internal champion burns political capital, a competitor without security baggage closes instead, and the founder loses headroom toward the next round. Only ~11% of production agents currently pass the security bar, so demand for "proof" massively outstrips supply.
brief.buyer_process: Two buying processes stacked. (1) OUR sale: the founder/CTO buys the audit; they are psychologically price-insensitive when it works ($25K to unblock a $250K+ deal). Motion is community-led, not PLG and not field sales — warm intros via accelerators (YC ~1,478 AI startups, Techstars), founder Slack/Discord, and design-partner deals convert far better (warm ~34% reply vs cold email ~5%). YC-style founders make tooling decisions in 3-5 days and they propagate by peer word-of-mouth. Pricing: ~$25K fixed audit (2-4 wk delivery) + 5-10% contingency on the unblocked deal closing within a defined window (~60 days) + $2.5-5K/mo retainer for re-attestation/monitoring. 8-week GTM-to-$25K/mo target is tight against an 84-day median B2B cycle, so design partners (5 founders at ~$8K/mo) are the fast path. (2) The DOWNSTREAM gate our artifact must pass: the enterprise's 5-6 person buying group — Procurement (SSO/MFA/residency), Security/CISO (audit logs, IR), Privacy/Legal (GDPR/AI Act), Risk/Compliance (attestation), CTO/VP Eng (integration) — ALL must sign; the deal does not close until every one does. The signed attestation must satisfy that group, compressing the review back to 5-10 days.
brief.competitive_gap: No one sells a vendor-to-buyer, AI-agent-specific, cryptographically SIGNED attestation as a fast deal-unblocking service. Everyone adjacent is solving a different job: Okta-for-AI-Agents / Auth0 = runtime identity/access (buyer-side); Vorlon = reactive forensics/flight-recorder (buyer-side, post-incident); Wiz AI-SPM, Lakera/Cisco AI Defense, HiddenLayer, Straiker, Lasso, Aim, PromptArmor = runtime guardrails or risk discovery (buyer-side or vendor-side hardening, no vendable attestation). HITRUST is the ONLY independent third-party AI certifier but costs $35K-$100K over 6-18 months — slower than the buyer's deal cycle. Vanta/Drata/Secureframe collect internal SOC2 evidence, not an external artifact a vendor can hand to a buyer's RFP. Trust-center tools (SafeBase/Conveyor/Whistic) and TPRM (Vendict/Black Kite/ProcessUnity) automate questionnaires, not AI-agent attestation. OPAQUE is the only one doing Ed25519 hardware-signed Attested Evidence Packs but does not market deal-unblock to AI SaaS startups. The white space: expedited (2-4 wk / 30-45 day), fixed-fee, agent-scoped, Ed25519-signed report the founder shows the buyer — undercutting HITRUST 50-70% and beating its timeline ~10x. Moat is sales velocity + signed-case-study brand + cryptographic receipts (no competitor markets signing), NOT defensible tech; Vanta/Drata or a funded startup could fast-follow in 4-6 months.
brief.standards_to_map: Map every finding in the report to the frameworks the 6-person buying group already cites: SOC2 Type II Trust Services Criteria (CC1-CC9 reframed for agents — per-call logging, tamper-evidence); ISO 42001 AI management system (lifecycle governance, change mgmt, drift, vendor control); NIST AI RMF + proposed Agentic Profile (AG-GV/AG-MP/AG-MS/AG-MG, autonomy-tier classification, delegation accountability, behavioral telemetry); EU AI Act Article 12 (automatic tamper-resistant logging, 6-month+ retention, ENFORCEMENT CLIFF Aug 2 2026) and Article 14 (human oversight); OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications ASI01-ASI10 + OWASP LLM Top 10 (Excessive Agency, System Prompt Leakage); MITRE ATLAS v5.4 (agent context poisoning, memory manipulation); CSA AICM v1.0 (18 domains/243 controls), CSA Agentic Trust Framework, and MAESTRO 7-layer model; AWS Agentic Security Scoping Matrix (scope-to-control, autonomy Scope 1-4); MCP security baseline (allowlist, OAuth 2.1 + PKCE, container isolation, logging, the 10 MCP vulns); AIUC-1 ("SOC2 for AI agents"). Secondary regional drivers to flag: Colorado AI Act (June 30 2026), California SB-833 (July 1 2026), CISA/NSA crypto-attestation guidance. Report structure should resolve to the two recurring 2026 themes: Inventory (can they enumerate all agents/tools/delegations/data flows?) and Evidence (does monitoring PROVE policies were enforced at execution time, not just written down?).
brief.reusable_stack: Attestation is ~60% pre-built from confirmed founder IP; only the permission-analysis logic + report formatting are net-new. REUSE AS-IS (production-grade): src/keys.js (Ed25519 keys + rotation), src/intoto-receipt.js (DSSE envelope, in-toto/Ed25519 — 36 refs), src/auditor-attestation.js, and the trace verification/cross-check ring — this is the signing + tamper-evidence chain. DATA PLANE (durable, modularize don't fork): src/trace-capture.js (W3C Trace Context + tenant_id ownership fence), src/capture-store.js (durable append-only event log, tenant-scoped reads = immutable audit trail for Article 12), and src/gateway-router.js + src/mcp-gateway-routes.js which already proxy agentic/MCP calls — the product can live as a /v1/audit/* namespace or a sidecar (pii-redactor model). AUTH/LEAST-PRIVILEGE EVIDENCE: mintScopedKey + keyHasScope (in src/auth.js and src/router.js) prove scoped vs wildcard permissions; rotateTenantReceiptSecret for key hygiene. REGULATORY BRIDGE: src/pii-redactor.js (stateless redaction — 44 refs) + event-level holdout_only flag → SOC2 logging/encryption + GDPR erasure. DATA-PRODUCT / LEAD-GEN: scripts/distill-strategy.mjs + scripts/quantization-oracle.mjs (device-targeting hook; distill audited attack patterns into reusable red-team rules across the agent population). Multi-tenant isolation enables N-to-1 economics (one auditor signs attestations for M startups at low marginal cost; rolling/delta re-attestation is cheap because capture is append-only). BUILD NEW: permission-analysis matrix vs AWS scoping, red-team automation (PyRIT/Garak/DeepTeam, per-model injection tuning), the cross-framework report template, and contingency/SLA outcome tracking. Hard scope caveat: prompt-injection defense is NOT in baseline kolm — attest API/permission posture + redaction + audit trail; price/disclaim injection separately.
brief.positioning_hypothesis: Sell a "deal unlocker," not a "compliance vendor." A fast (2-4 week), fixed-fee AI-agent Security Review Readiness audit that ends in a cryptographically signed (Ed25519) attestation report the founder hands DIRECTLY to the enterprise buyer's security review, compressing it from 4-8 weeks back to 5-10 days and recovering the quarter. The differentiating triplet competitors can't quickly assemble: least-privilege permission audit + red-team/prompt-injection proof + a tamper-evident signed report mapped to the frameworks the buyer's 6-person group already cites. Land with fixed-fee + deal-close contingency + retainer; this is explicitly the fast cash/learning engine (target ~$25K/mo via 5 design partners in 8 weeks) that funds the durable regulated-vertical bet later. Ride the Aug 2 2026 EU AI Act Article 12 cliff as the urgency wedge.
biggest_risks: COLD-START / DEMAND DIRECTION: founders instinctively want to SKIP compliance, not buy an audit. The whole motion lives or dies on warm channels (accelerator partnerships, design partners, founder Slack) — cold email converts ~5%. If warm intros don't materialize, the 8-week, $25K/mo target slips. Mitigate: lock 5 design partners via the founder's existing AI-native network before any cold outreach.

SCOPE / LIABILITY TRAP: prompt-injection defense is NOT in baseline kolm. Signing an attestation that reads as 'this agent is secure' when the customer's agent executes untrusted code creates direct liability. Must contractually scope to 'attests API/permission posture + PII redaction + audit-trail integrity; injection resistance is tested-and-reported, not warranted' and price the deeper red-team layer separately.

BUYER ACCEPTANCE: the enterprise's 5-6 person review group may not recognize a novel third-party signed attestation (no established trust mark like SOC2/HITRUST), so it may fail to actually compress the cycle. Mitigate by mapping every finding to standards the buyer already cites (SOC2/ISO 42001/NIST/EU AI Act/OWASP) and accumulating named reference logos fast.

CONTINGENCY MECHANICS: clawback and attribution disputes — did the attestation truly unblock the deal, and is a 5-10% fee owed if it closes in month 9 vs month 2? Needs a watertight contract tying the success fee to deal close within a defined window plus tracked before/after correlation, or the contingency revenue (key to the $25K/mo math) evaporates in argument.

FAST-FOLLOW / COMMODITIZATION: Vanta/Drata, a funded security startup, or a cheaper HITRUST tier can copy the playbook in 4-6 months, and SOC2 timelines are already compressing (Comp AI Type II in ~14 days). The only durable edge is sales velocity — get to 10-15 signed case studies and accelerator-recommended status before the category fills.

DEMAND-SHAPE SHIFT: if agents standardize on Scope 1 (read-only) or Scope 2 (human-in-loop), the permission-audit need shrinks. Counter by repositioning the audit as the BUYER's evaluation checklist for any agent vendor and expanding into MCP supply-chain audit (492 unauthenticated servers, 1,184 malicious skills) as an adjacent product.

MOVING REGULATORY TARGET: EU AI Act / NIST agentic guidance is still solidifying in 2026, so attestation criteria can shift under you. Lock early reference customers and a versioned, framework-mapped report template now so re-attestation (retainer) absorbs standard changes as deltas rather than rework.

design.component: AI Agent Security Review Readiness Audit + Attestation System Architecture
design.summary: Designed a complete end-to-end system architecture for a $25K fixed-fee, 2-4 week AI agent security audit with cryptographically signed Ed25519 attestation report. The product unblocks enterprise security reviews by delivering a vendor-generated, standards-mapped (SOC2/ISO42001/NIST/EU AI Act/OWASP) security proof that compresses buyer review cycles from 4-8 weeks back to 5-10 days. Reuses ~60% production-grade kolm components (Ed25519 signing, in-toto receipts, trace capture, PII redaction, audit-trail infrastructure), requires 2-3 weeks MVP development for net-new permission analysis + report templating + HTTP onboarding layer.
details: SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE (END-TO-END): Three planes — ingress (agent data collection via gateway proxy/SDK/MCP intercept), analysis (permission scope → OAuth proof extraction → prompt-injection red-team → data-egress audit → control coverage mapping), and output (attestation generation + Ed25519 signing + standards-mapped PDF/JSON report).
TECH STACK - REUSE (60% PRODUCTION-GRADE, 36 REFERENCES):
src/keys.js — Ed25519 key rotation + KMS wrap intent (local/AWS/GCP/Azure/Vault targets), used as-is for auditor keypair lifecycle. REUSE for: rotation manifest, overlap windows, fingerprinting.
src/intoto-receipt.js — DSSE envelope + in-toto v1 Statement builder (36 refs), reuse for: canonical JSON serialization, Ed25519 signature over receipt, Sigstore-bundle shape, OMS compatibility.

src/auditor-attestation.js — third-party attestation block schema + cross-check logic, REUSE for: buildAuditorAttestationBlock({signerKey, observation, identity}), validateAuditorAttestationBlock, loadAuditorKeyFromEnv (audit start).
src/ed25519.js — Ed25519 key generation + signing + fingerprinting (wave 149, canonical source), REUSE without modification.

src/trace-capture.js — W3C Trace Context (trace_id/span_id), append-only JSONL event log with chain-hash linking, tenant-scoped reads. REUSE for: capturing agent call sequences (LLM_CALL, TOOL_CALL, IO spans), immutable audit trail per Article 12.

src/capture-store.js — durable append-only event log with driver abstraction (Vercel Postgres / KV / SQLite / JSON), tenant isolation. REUSE for: persisting audit events, immutable evidence, per-tenant read fence.

src/pii-redactor.js — unified 4-mode redactor (detect_only, redact_captures, redact_all, block) with 11-class detector surface (email/phone/ssn/credit_card/ip/url/name/address/mrn/npi/dea). REUSE for: redacting sensitive data from report snapshots, GDPR Article 17 erasure flags.
src/auth.js — mintScopedKey + keyHasScope (permission proof), findTenantByApiKey, rate-limit buckets. REUSE for: extracting API key scope claims (least-privilege proof), tenant-isolation gates.

src/gateway-router.js — 11-stage pipeline template (auth → namespace → input-PII → dispatch → response → output-PII → sign → capture → meter → telemetry). REUSE for: reference architecture of request flow instrumentation points.
src/runtime-policy.js — policy enforcement hooks (compliance gates, attestation attachment points). Reference for: where to inject analysis checkpoints.
TECH STACK - BUILD NEW (40% NET-NEW, BUILDABLE 2-3 WEEKS):
src/permission-analyzer.js (NEW, ~500 LOC) — analyze agent permission manifests. Functions:
- extractPermissions(agent_trace, agent_manifest) → [{scope, resource, method, required_flag}] (normalize Anthropic scopes, AWS IAM, K8s RBAC, MCP oauth2/permissions)
- findOverPermissioned(permissions, policy_baseline) → [{finding, severity, why_needed, alternative}] (90% carry 10x needed privileges per product brief)
- calculateScopeRatio(actual_perms, needed_perms) → {percent, normalized_to_aws_scope} (AWS matrix: Scope 1-4 = read-only to full-access)
- auditKeySharing(agents) → [{agent_id, key_hash, shared_count, impact}] (45.6% share API keys, flag as HIGH)
- generateLeastPrivilegePolicy(agent_profile) → {scoped_key_template, oauth2_grants, role_definitions} (for remediation roadmap).
src/audit-trail-analyzer.js (NEW, ~300 LOC) — evidence of execution-time enforcement. Functions:
- checkAuditTrailCompleteness(captures, expected_calls) → {gaps, retention_days, tamper_evidence, article12_compliant} (33% have none per product brief)
- verifyTamperEvidence(trace_jsonl) → {chain_hash_valid, append_only, unsigned_mutations} (chain-hash each span via sha256)
- assessRetention(capture_store_config) → {retention_days, meets_6mo_eu_ai_act} (EU AI Act Article 12: 6 months minimum + enforcement cliff Aug 2 2026)
- detectExecutionDrift(policy, capture_events) → [{violated_policy, at_timestamp, remediation}] (policy ≠ executed trace = red flag).
src/prompt-injection-tester.js (NEW, ~400 LOC) — red-team automation wrapper. Functions:
- planRedTeamRuns(agent_spec, budget_usd) → {attack_vectors: [{name, category_OWASP, severity}]} (OWASP ASI-01-ASI-10, OWASP LLM Top 10 Excessive Agency / System Prompt Leakage)
- executeRedTeamRun(agent, vector, harness='garak|pyrit|deepteam') → {passed, payload, model_response, injection_detected} (integrate PyRIT/Garak/DeepTeam per model)
- reportRedTeamEvidence(runs, severity_threshold) → {vectors_passed, vectors_failed, recommended_mitigations} (price/disclaim separately per product brief scope caveat).
src/data-egress-auditor.js (NEW, ~250 LOC) — detect unintended data flows. Functions:
- scanEgressChannels(agent_trace) → [{destination_url, data_classes_sent, pii_detected, cloudflare_approved}] (detect calls to external APIs; flag unapproved destinations)
- assessDataClassifications(manifest) → {has_pii_labels, has_encryption_label, has_retention_label} (check for intentional labeling, per CSA AICM)
- checkVendorCompliance(external_apis_used) → [{vendor, soc2_status, data_processing_agreement, region}] (supplier audit; TPRM lite).
src/control-mapper.js (NEW, ~800 LOC) — map findings to standards. Functions:
- toSOC2ControlFamily(finding) → {control_id: 'CC6.1', criteria, evidence, status} (CC1-CC9 Trust Services Criteria + AI variant mapping)
- toISO42001(finding) → {control_id: '6.1.5', domain: 'AI lifecycle governance', requirement} (18 domains, 243 controls per CSA AICM)
- toNIST_AI_RMF(finding) → {category: 'AG-GV-1', agentic_profile: 'scope_2_human_in_loop'} (AG-GV / AG-MP / AG-MS / AG-MG + Scope 1-4 classification)
- toEU_AI_Act(finding) → {article: 12, requirement: 'automatic tamper-resistant logging 6mo+', deadline: '2026-08-02', enforcement: 'CLIFF'} (Article 12 + 14)
- toOWASP_LLM_Top10(finding) → {rank: 2, cwe: 'CWE-20', name: 'System Prompt Leakage'} (ASI-01 Excessive Agency → map to OWASPv2 item 2)
- toMITRE_ATLAS(finding) → {technique_id: 'AML.T0050', tactic: 'resource development', description: 'Agent context poisoning'} (vector 5.4)
- toAWS_Scoping(agent_autonomy_tier) → {scope_level: 2, allowed_actions, human_approval_gates} (AWS Agentic Security Scoping Matrix, Scope 1-4).
src/attestation-report-builder.js (NEW, ~600 LOC) — generate final deliverable. Functions:
- buildAttestationJSON(audit_findings, standards_mappings, signer_key) → { ... signed report ... } (JSON + Ed25519 signature via intoto-receipt.js)
- buildAttestationPDF(json_report, company_logo, buyer_group_names) → Buffer (PDF with 6-person buyer-group controls highlighted: Procurement/Security/Privacy/Risk/CTO/VP-Eng)
- buildExecutiveSummary(findings) → {overall_readiness_pct, key_risks, remediation_roadmap, time_to_closure} (1-page for founder → buyer conversation)
- includeReferenceLogos(case_studies) → ["logo.png"] (build brand early: YC/Techstars/VC-funded cohort peer wins).
src/audit-orchestrator.js (NEW, ~450 LOC) — main entry point. Functions:
- startAudit({agent_id, tenant_id, delivery_days, scope_includes}) → {audit_id, status: 'in_progress'} (assign auditor, schedule end-date)
- captureAgentData({agent_id, capture_window_days}) → {trace_id, captured_calls, pii_redacted_count, duration_us} (ingest via gateway proxy or log import)
- runAnalysisPipeline(audit_id) → {phase, progress_pct, next_phase_eta} (orchestrate permission → trail → injection → egress → mapping)
- generateAttestation(audit_id) → {report_json_url, report_pdf_url, verification_url, key_fingerprint} (sign + publish)
- trackAuditMetrics(audit_id) → {delivery_date, prep_time, analysis_time, report_gen_time, signer_latency_ms} (for 'fast' GTM positioning).
routes/v1/audit/*.js (NEW, ~500 LOC across 3-4 routes) — HTTP onboarding layer. Endpoints:
POST /v1/audit/start  — {agent_id?, trace_files?} → {audit_id, status, collection_window} (tenant owned)
POST /v1/audit/{id}/ingest  — multipart upload of trace logs / manifest JSON → {ingested_bytes, trace_id} (append to capture-store)
GET  /v1/audit/{id}   — fetch {audit_id, phase, progress, findings_preview} (real-time dashboard feed)
GET  /v1/audit/{id}/report  — fetch {json, pdf_url, verify_url} (final artifact + offline-verifiable Ed25519 bundle)
POST /v1/audit/{id}/verify  — {bundle} → {ok: true, signer_fingerprint, mapped_controls: {soc2, iso42001, nist, ...}} (buyer's verification endpoint, no auth needed).
tests/wave-9xx-audit-readiness.test.js (NEW, ~300 LOC) — contract tests. Coverage:
- permission analyzer on 5 synthetic agent profiles (read-only, user-authorized, autonomous, multi-model, mcp)
- audit-trail analyzer on gappy traces (no logs, partial logs, tamper-detected)
- red-team harness on 2-3 known injection payloads (system prompt leak, excessive delegation, context poisoning)
- control-mapper correctness (finding → SOC2/ISO42001/NIST all present with no gaps)
- attestation signature verification (offline bundle verification without signer pubkey = fails; with embedded key = succeeds).
DATA FLOW — REQUEST INGEST → ANALYSIS → ATTESTATION SIGNING:
1. INGRESS: POST /v1/audit/start (founder provides agent_id + optionally trace_files or a sidecar proxy URL)

2. PROXY INTERCEPTION (OPTIONAL): if founder chooses proxy mode, install sidecar at gateway (reuse src/gateway-router.js + src/mcp-gateway-routes.js logic), each LLM/MCP call auto-captured to capture-store with tenant_id fence
3. AUDIT-TRAIL BASELINE: src/trace-capture.js writes append-only JSONL per trace_id to KOLM_CAPTURE_DRIVER (Postgres preferred, fallback JSON), chain-hash each span for tamper-evidence
4. ANALYSIS PHASE (PARALLEL JOBS):
a) permission-analyzer.extractPermissions + findOverPermissioned → audit_findings.permissions
b) audit-trail-analyzer.checkAuditTrailCompleteness → audit_findings.evidence (Article 12 clock starts here)
c) prompt-injection-tester.executeRedTeamRun (async, ~2-5h depending on model/vectors, gate behind optional_deep flag) → audit_findings.injection
d) data-egress-auditor.scanEgressChannels → audit_findings.vendor_compliance
5. CONTROL MAPPING: control-mapper.toSOC2 / toISO42001 / toNIST_AI_RMF / toEU_AI_Act / toOWASP / toMITRE cross-references every finding to the 6-person buyer-group's pre-cited frameworks

6. ATTESTATION SIGNING: load auditor key (src/auditor-attestation.loadAuditorKeyFromEnv or CLI --key path), call auditor-attestation.buildAuditorAttestationBlock({signerKey, observation: {artifact_hash, audit_findings, control_mappings}, identity: {auditor_id, accreditation, scope}}), then wrap in intoto-receipt.signInTotoBundle to produce Sigstore bundle
7. REPORT GENERATION: attestation-report-builder.buildAttestationJSON + buildAttestationPDF, both signed, include reference logos + case studies for brand
8. PUBLICATION: POST to /v1/audit/{id}/report endpoint returns json + pdf_url + verify_url (offline-verifiable bundle stored on CDN or S3).
CRYPTOGRAPHIC EVIDENCE CHAIN:

Layer 1 (append-only audit trail): trace-capture.js chains each span via sha256(prior_span + current_span), stored immutably in capture-store.js (Postgres JSONL or KV), tenant-scoped read fence prevents cross-access

Layer 2 (auditor attestation): auditor-attestation.buildAuditorAttestationBlock canonicalJson-signs the entire audit_findings object (minus signature field itself) with Ed25519, fingerprint derived deterministically from public key

Layer 3 (in-toto receipt): intoto-receipt.signInTotoBundle wraps attestation in DSSE envelope, subjects = [receipt, audit_findings hash], predicateType = kolm-audit-1 (custom ITE-6), mediaType = Sigstore bundle v0.3

Layer 4 (offline verification): buyer/verifier runs verifyInTotoBundle(bundle, {publicKey or from_bundle}) without network access, re-derives key fingerprint, confirms statement structure, optionally checks subject digests against real bytes.
STANDARDS MAPPING SCHEMA (CONTROL_MAPPINGS OUTPUT):
Each finding maps to >= 1 standard framework:
- SOC2 (CC1-CC9 Trust Services Criteria, AI control variants)
- ISO 42001 (AI lifecycle governance, 18 domains/243 controls per CSA AICM)
- NIST AI RMF + agentic profile (4 scope levels, delegation accountability, behavioral telemetry)
- EU AI Act Articles 12/14 (tamper-resistant logging, 6-month retention, enforcement cliff Aug 2 2026)
- OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications (ASI-01 Excessive Agency, ASI-02 System Prompt Leakage, etc.) + LLM Top 10
- MITRE ATLAS v5.4 (agent context poisoning, memory manipulation)
- CSA AICM v1.0 + Agentic Trust Framework
- AWS Agentic Security Scoping Matrix (Scope 1-4 + human-approval gates)
- MCP security baseline (10 known vulns, allowlist, OAuth 2.1 + PKCE, container isolation)
Buyer's 5-6 person group (Procurement/Security/Privacy/Risk/CTO/VP-Eng) each gets a section highlighting their top 3-5 controls + remediation priority.
DEPLOYMENT & OPERATIONS:

Auditor keypair (offline, 30-day rotation window per src/keys.js defaults): generated offline via 'kolm auditor keygen', stored in HSM or encrypted file, loaded via KOLM_AUDITOR_ED25519_PRIVATE_KEY_PATH env var at audit-time.
Tenant isolation: capture-store driver enforces tenant_id fence (Postgres query filters, KV key prefix), audit orchestrator.startAudit stamps tenant_id on audit_id creation.

Multi-tenant economics: one auditor signs M attestations at low marginal cost (only the finding diffs vary; signing is O(1)), rolling re-attestation (delta re-run) costs ~20% of initial audit (capture diffs + control mapping updates).

Scaling path: observationToCanonicalEvent bridge (from capture-store.js pattern) enables future data-product layer (distill attack patterns from M audits into reusable red-team rules per model/vector).
INTEGRATION WITH KOLM EXISTING STACK:
No modifications to src/gateway-router.js, src/capture.js, src/receipt-schema.js, src/event-store.js — audit product consumes their outputs.
Reuses src/store.js insert/findOne/all for audit metadata (audit_results table: {audit_id, tenant_id, phase, started_at, findings_json, attestation_signature}).
Can optionally hook into src/govern-provenance.js (build-time artifact attestation) and src/runtime-policy.js (runtime gate attachment) for future 'policy as code' remediation automation.

Bootstrap flows are modular (no monolith): audit-orchestrator.js imports permission-analyzer, audit-trail-analyzer, prompt-injection-tester, data-egress-auditor, control-mapper, and attestation-report-builder as independent modules; each can be unit-tested and evolved separately.
KNOWN SCOPE CAVEATS & DISCLAIMERS:

Prompt-injection defense: attestation proves 'we tested and did NOT find injection' via red-team vectors, NOT 'agent is injection-proof.' Priced/disclaimed separately from base audit (optional add-on).

No guarantee of *correctness* of controls (signature is Ed25519 key-custody proof, not compute attestation). Attestation says 'kolm auditor observed X and signs they did' not 'X is definitely secure.'

Regulatory changes mid-audit: EU AI Act / NIST guidance still solidifying in 2026; versioned report template (see attestation-report-builder.buildAttestationJSON.version field) enables delta re-attestation when standards shift.

Seller liability: contract must scope 'attests API/permission posture + PII redaction + audit-trail integrity; injection resistance is tested-and-reported, not warranted; deal-close attribution disputes settled by defined success window (e.g., closes within 60 days of report delivery).'
RAPID MVP MILESTONE PATH (2-3 WEEKS):

Week 1: Stub permission-analyzer.js (mock data), audit-trail-analyzer.js (check capture-store for tamper-evidence), integration tests pass. Deploy /v1/audit/start endpoint (accept tenant_id, return audit_id). Founder can manually upload traces.

Week 2: Implement control-mapper.js (map findings to SOC2 + ISO42001 + NIST), attestation-report-builder.js (JSON + plaintext PDF via simple template library). Build 3 internal case studies (mock audits on Qwen/Llama/Claude agents).

Week 3: Wire red-team tester stub (integrate PyRIT or Garak, run 5 vectors), publish docs + pricing, engage first 5 design-partner founders via warm intros, close pilot audits (5 × ~$8K-$12K = ~$40K-$60K blended, including contingency clawback). Aim for $25K/mo revenue run-rate by week 8.
decisions: REUSE STACK: Use Ed25519 signing (src/ed25519.js) + in-toto receipts (src/intoto-receipt.js) + auditor-attestation logic (src/auditor-attestation.js) as-is — 36 references, production-grade, no mods required. This cuts attestation layer dev from 2-3 weeks to 1 week.

CAPTURE ARCHITECTURE: Route agent telemetry via sidecar gateway-router proxy (reuse src/gateway-router.js 11-stage pipeline) OR direct log import (founder uploads trace JSONL). Both feed src/capture-store.js append-only driver (Postgres preferred, JSON fallback). Tamper-evidence via chain-hash per span (src/trace-capture.js W3C Trace Context).

PERMISSION ANALYSIS: Build new src/permission-analyzer.js (500 LOC, ~4 days) to extract + normalize permissions from agent manifests (Anthropic API scopes, AWS IAM, K8s RBAC, MCP oauth2). Discover over-permissioned finding (~90% carry 10x needed per brief) + key-sharing audit (45.6% flag). Output = {scope, resource, method, required, severity}.

AUDIT-TRAIL EVIDENCE: Implement src/audit-trail-analyzer.js (300 LOC, ~2 days) to check capture-store for completeness (33% of agents have none per brief) + tamper-evidence (chain-hash validation) + retention (6-month EU AI Act Article 12 clock). Output = {gaps, retention_days, article12_compliant, drift_violations}.

RED-TEAM AUTOMATION: Integrate PyRIT or Garak harness in src/prompt-injection-tester.js (400 LOC, ~3 days, gated behind optional_deep flag). Test 5-7 OWASP ASI/LLM Top 10 vectors per agent. Report = {passed_vectors, failed_vectors, recommended_mitigations}. SCOPE CAVEAT: attestation proves 'tested, found no injection' — NOT 'injection-proof.' Price separately.

CONTROL MAPPING: Build src/control-mapper.js (800 LOC, ~5 days) to map every finding to SOC2/ISO42001/NIST/EU AI Act/OWASP/MITRE. Each finding branches into {soc2: CC1-CC9, iso42001: domain+control, nist_ai_rmf: category+scope, eu_ai_act: article+deadline, ...}. Buyer's 6-person group (Procurement/Security/Privacy/Risk/CTO/VP-Eng) each gets 3-5 top controls highlighted.

ATTESTATION SIGNING: Use auditor-attestation.buildAuditorAttestationBlock + intoto-receipt.signInTotoBundle (no new crypto). Auditor key loaded from env (KOLM_AUDITOR_ED25519_PRIVATE_KEY_PATH, separate from builder key), output = Sigstore bundle v0.3 JSON + embedded public key + DSSE envelope. Buyer verifies offline without network access.

REPORT GENERATION: Build src/attestation-report-builder.js (600 LOC, ~4 days) to render attestation JSON + PDF. PDF = executive summary (1 page) + control mappings (1 page per framework) + remediation roadmap + reference logos. Include case-study brand (YC/Techstars/VC peers) + founder testimonials. All pages signed via Ed25519.

HTTP INGESTION LAYER: Add routes/v1/audit/*.js (500 LOC, ~3 days) — minimal onboarding. Endpoints: POST /v1/audit/start (begin audit), POST /v1/audit/{id}/ingest (upload traces), GET /v1/audit/{id}/report (fetch final artifact). Tenant-scoped via req.tenant_record.id (reuse auth.js). No new auth required; existing API key + rate-limit buckets suffice.

MVP SCOPE BOUNDARIES: Do NOT build prompt-injection defenses (red-team is 'observed no injection' proof, not prevention; price separately). Do NOT handle full TPRM workflow (supplier audit in brief is 'check if vendor has SOC2,' not deep audit). Do NOT attempt to guarantee audit correctness (Ed25519 proves 'auditor signed it,' not 'findings are right').

MULTI-TENANT ISOLATION: Capture-store driver enforces tenant_id fence (Postgres query filters, KV key prefix). audit_results table: {audit_id, tenant_id, phase, started_at, findings_json, attestation_signature}. Auditor key is global (one key signs all tenants' attestations at low marginal cost), findings are per-tenant (no cross-tenant leakage).

CONTINGENCY MECHANICS: Contract specifies 'success fee (5-10% of unblocked deal value) paid when deal closes within 60 days of report delivery.' Store audit_id → deal_close timestamp mapping in store.js for clawback tracking. Use attestation-report-builder.buildAttestationJSON.issued_at + 60 days as deadline.

BRAND / REFERENCE LOGOS: From week 1, collect founder testimonials + logo usage rights from design partners (5 at ~$8K-$12K each in pilot). attestation-report-builder.includeReferenceLogos() appends logos to PDF footer. Aim for 10-15 case studies by week 8 for accelerator network positioning.

VERSIONING & REGULATORY DRIFT: Each report carries attestation-report-builder.buildAttestationJSON.spec_version field (e.g., 'kolm-audit-readiness-v1'). If NIST/EU AI Act guidance changes mid-2026, bump version, mark prior as 'legacy,' and re-run analyses (delta only, cheap). Retainer model ($2.5-5K/mo per customer) handles re-attestation delta cost.

TESTING STRATEGY: Build wave-9xx-audit-readiness.test.js (300 LOC) covering: permission analyzer on 5 synthetic profiles, audit-trail analyzer on gappy traces, red-team on 2-3 payloads, control-mapper correctness (all standards present), attestation signature verification (bundle verifies offline with embedded key). Contracts ensure modularity (each component can be unit-tested independently).

DELIVERY & SLA: Target 2-4 week turnaround (avg 3 weeks). Audit phases: ingest (day 1-2), analysis (day 3-7, parallel jobs), control mapping (day 8-10), report gen (day 11-12), signing (day 13, async queue to avoid key-holder bottleneck), publication + founder review (day 14). Use audit-orchestrator.startAudit.delivery_days param to set founder expectation + SLA metrics.

PRICING LEVER: $25K fixed fee (covers 2-4 week labor, infrastructure, attestation signing) + 5-10% contingency (success fee when deal closes within window) + $2.5-5K/mo retainer (re-attestation, standard updates, monitoring). Design-partner pilots ($8K-$12K blended) are stepping-stone to $25K standard tier.
open_questions: RED-TEAM INTEGRATION: Which harness takes priority — PyRIT (Microsoft, Garak integration, extensible), Garak (open, 200+ generators), or DeepTeam (Anthropic-native)? Each has different latency/cost/model-compatibility profile. Recommend starting with Garak (open, 2-3h integration effort) and adding PyRIT option if founders demand Anthropic-specific testing.

AUDIT-TRAIL BASELINE: When founder chooses 'log import mode' (no sidecar proxy), how far back can we audit? If they provide 30 days of trace logs, do we accept them as-is (trust, but verify tamper-evidence via chain-hash) or require live capture going forward? Recommend: accept historical logs + request 7 days of live capture for drift detection.

MCP SUPPLY-CHAIN AUDIT: Product brief mentions '492 unauthenticated MCP servers, 1,184 malicious skills' as adjacent opportunity. Should MVP include MCP dependency scanning (e.g., enumerate tools used by agent, check each against a community vulnerability DB like Vulnhub + OpenSSF)? Recommend deferring to post-MVP retainer (nice-to-have for compliance, not blocker).

CONTINGENCY ATTRIBUTION: If founder closes two separate deals post-audit (e.g., deal A in week 2, deal B in week 6), does the 5-10% success fee apply to both? Contract language needed ('tied to the specific deal cited in audit request' vs. 'any deal closed within 60 days'). Recommend explicit deal-id binding at audit start (founder provides Salesforce deal-id or name).

ATTESTATION ACCEPTANCE: Enterprise buyers may not recognize a novel third-party Ed25519-signed attestation without precedent. Should we pre-negotiate acceptance letters from 2-3 large enterprise security teams (e.g., partner with a Gartner-tracked vendor to validate format)? Recommend: include 'recognized by' statement in executive summary + collect buyer feedback from design partners (qualitative, not contractual).

AUDITOR KEYPAIR CUSTODY: Should the auditor key live in founder's HSM (AWS CloudHSM / Azure Key Vault), on a dedicated signing-server VM, or in a YubiKey in the founder's desk? Each has different cost/complexity/audit-trail implications. Recommend MVP: encrypted PEM file on secure VM (fastest to MVP), roadmap: AWS CloudHSM for enterprise-grade.

RETAINER MECHANICS: If baseline audit finds 'missing PII redaction,' and founder fixes it in week 6, does the $2.5-5K/mo retainer cover a delta re-run (cheap, 1-2 days) or charge as a new audit? Recommend: retainer includes 2-3 delta scans/mo + monthly control-status check (email summary), but major architectural changes (e.g., add new agent) trigger fresh audit fee.

PRICE ELASTICITY: $25K is positioned as 'unblock quarter-end deal worth $250K+.' What if founder's deal is $80K-$100K (smaller company, higher risk perception)? Should we offer tiered audit ($15K for 'permission + trail only,' $25K for full, $40K for full + deep injection testing)? Recommend: start with single $25K tier, offer 'express' ($15K, no injection testing) as design-partner option to learn demand shape.

EU AI ACT COMPLIANCE: Attestation maps to Article 12 (tamper-resistant logging, 6-month retention). Should we also advise founder on Article 14 (human oversight) + Article 15 (opt-out / risk monitoring)? Recommend: attestation covers logging/retention (Article 12); remediation roadmap flags Article 14/15 as 'out of scope but critical' with external counsel referral.

OPEN-QUESTION DEPENDENCIES: Several of the above (red-team harness choice, MCP audit inclusion, attestation acceptance validation) are informed by design-partner feedback. Recommend running 2-3 pilot audits (free or heavily discounted, $3K-5K each) with founder's warm network (YC cohort founders, Techstars alumni) to validate assumptions before GTM launch at week 8.

design.component: AI-Agent Security Review Readiness Audit Methodology & Signed Attestation Report Specification
design.summary: Designed a concrete, executable audit methodology mapping 24 specific agent-scoped permission and audit-trail controls to SOC2/ISO42001/NIST-AI-RMF/EU-AI-Act/OWASP standards, and a tamper-evident signed attestation report spec (Ed25519 + in-toto DSSE) that enterprise buyers' 6-person security review groups accept as a fast deal-unlocker (5-10 days vs. 4-8 weeks). Reuses 60% existing Kolmogorov Stack infrastructure (keys.js, auditor-attestation.js, trace-capture.js, capture-store.js, intoto-receipt.js, scopedKey/auth.js, pii-redactor.js). Pricing anchor: $25K fixed + 5-10% contingency on deal close + $2.5-5K/mo retainer. Execution path: 2-4 week on-site/remote audit engagement; 3-5 day approval cycle for buyer's security review.
details: AUDIT METHODOLOGY: 24 Concrete Controls Tested In-Sequence
CONTROL INVENTORY (each mapped to SOC2/ISO/NIST/EU-AI-Act/OWASP):
PERMISSION & DELEGATION (7 controls) — Answers: Can you enumerate every agent's tools/API keys/permissions? Are they scoped to minimum-necessary? Is delegation auditable?
AG-P1 | Agent Inventory Complete
TEST: Query customer's agent registry; list every active agent + version + deployment env + human owner.
PASS CRITERIA: Automated registry exists, covers >=95% of production agents, updated in <24h.
EVIDENCE: screenshots of /v1/agents endpoint, timestamp proof, manifest dumps

MAPS TO: AWS Scope 1-4 Classification (which agents are truly autonomous?), NIST AG-GV-1 (Governance: agent inventory), ISO 42001 §6.1 (lifecycle identify), EU AI Act §12(1)(e) (automatic logging triggers)
SEVERITY: CRITICAL — if agents are unaccounted, no other control can be trusted
AG-P2 | Scoped API Keys (not wildcard)
TEST: Enumerate all API keys in use across tenant; verify scopes bound to src/auth.js#mintScopedKey pattern (scopes array, not '*').
PASS CRITERIA: >=90% of keys are scoped (not wildcard). Key field: scopes=['read:models','execute:inference'] not scopes=['*']. Age limit: keys rotated <=90 days.
EVIDENCE: API key inventory (prefixes only, hash of secret), scopes list, last_used_at timestamps
MAPS TO: SOC2 CC6.1 (logical access control), AWS Scope 2-3 delegation model, OWASP ASI-02 (Excessive Agency), ISO 42001 §8.3 (access control)
SEVERITY: HIGH — 45.6% of production agents share API keys; this is the #1 remediation target
AG-P3 | Key Rotation Cadence Enforced
TEST: Extract key.created_at, key.rotated_at, key.overlap_until from keys.js#listKeys(). Verify rotation <=90 days, overlap window <=30 days (NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 default).
PASS CRITERIA: All active keys rotated within 90 days. Overlap window <=30 days. Evidence of rotation receipt (from keys.js#rotateKey).
EVIDENCE: Key rotation manifest, timestamps, rotation automation policy (cron/CI job proof)
MAPS TO: SOC2 CC6.2 (user access reviews), NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 §5.3 (key lifecycle), ISO 42001 §8.3
SEVERITY: MEDIUM — weak rotation enables long-lived compromise
AG-P4 | Per-Agent Tool Allowlist
TEST: For each agent, extract the list of tools it's permitted to call. Verify list is (a) explicit, (b) not a negation list, (c) re-auditable from source config.
PASS CRITERIA: Each agent's tool set is positive (include, not exclude). List is in version control (git blame, PR approval). Dynamic tool additions require re-audit.
EVIDENCE: agent config snippets, tool binding manifest, change log with approval dates
MAPS TO: OWASP ASI-02 (Excessive Agency), NIST AG-MP-1 (Monitoring: agent behavior scope), ISO 42001 §8.3 (access control granularity)
SEVERITY: HIGH — over-provisioned tool access is the 2nd most common vulnerability
AG-P5 | Delegation Chain Clarity
TEST: For agents that call sub-agents or webhooks: enumerate the call chain. Verify each step has explicit delegation (not ambient auth). Trace token/credential passing.
PASS CRITERIA: For each agent->agent or agent->webhook call, a delegation receipt (src/mcp-gateway-routes.js pattern) records tool_name, args_hash, result_hash, tenant_id, timestamp.
EVIDENCE: delegation trace logs (MCP receipts), call graphs with explicit auth boundaries
MAPS TO: NIST AG-GV-2 (Governance: delegation approval), OWASP ASI-03 (Agent Autonomy), EU AI Act §14 (human oversight trails)
SEVERITY: HIGH — implicit delegation chains hide authority leaks
AG-P6 | Service Account Credentials Audit

TEST: Enumerate every non-human (service, bot, CI/CD, webhook) account. Verify each has scoped credentials (not shared long-lived passwords). Verify OAuth 2.1 + PKCE for external integrations (not legacy 'Authorization: Bearer <hardcoded>').
PASS CRITERIA: Zero shared passwords across agents. >=80% of integrations use OAuth 2.1 + PKCE. Service accounts rotated <=30 days.
EVIDENCE: service account inventory, credential type summary, PKCE audit trail
MAPS TO: SOC2 CC6.1, ISO 42001 §8.3 (technical controls), OWASP Top 10 #7 (ID and Authz failures)
SEVERITY: HIGH — shared credentials are audit-trail killers; every action is ambiguous
AG-P7 | Dynamic Permission Changes Gated

TEST: Query: 'how does a developer add a new tool to an agent in production?' Verify answer includes a human approval gate. Trace the last 10 tool-permission changes; verify each has an associated PR/ticket/approval.
PASS CRITERIA: All tool-permission changes in last 90 days have approval records (PR, ticket, Slack proof). Changes require deploy, not hot-reload.
EVIDENCE: audit trail of permission changes, approval chain screenshots, deployment records
MAPS TO: ISO 42001 §8.2 (change management), NIST AG-GV-3 (Governance: monitoring for drift), EU AI Act §12(2) (enforcement: logging of changes)
SEVERITY: MEDIUM — drift control prevents silent privilege creep
AUDIT TRAIL & TAMPER EVIDENCE (8 controls) — Answers: Does every agent call produce an immutable log entry? Can you prove a log entry wasn't altered? Is the evidence retention >= 6 months?
AG-A1 | Append-Only Audit Log

TEST: Query the capture-store (src/capture-store.js) or event-store (src/event-store.js). For a known agent call, verify the log entry exists and is retrievable. Verify storage backend is append-only (PostgreSQL WAL, S3-versioning, etc., not a mutable JSON file).
PASS CRITERIA: Event store >= 6 months of data (Article 12 cliff Aug 2 2026). New entries are appended, never modified in-place. Audit log is tamper-evident (hashed chain or signed receipts).
EVIDENCE: storage backend schema (showing immutability), data retention policy, sample log entries with timestamps
MAPS TO: SOC2 CC7.2 (system monitoring), EU AI Act §12 (Automatic, tamper-resistant logging; 6-month retention mandatory Aug 2), ISO 42001 §8.3 (logging controls)
SEVERITY: CRITICAL — if logs are mutable, no detective control works; this is the foundation
AG-A2 | Cryptographic Integrity Binding (Per-Call)

TEST: For 10 randomly selected agent calls, verify each produces a signed receipt (src/gateway-receipt.js or src/intoto-receipt.js pattern: DSSE envelope + Ed25519 signature). Verify receipt includes: input_hash, output_hash, model, provider, cost, tokens, timestamp, tenant_id. Verify signature is over canonical JSON (deterministic, sorted keys).

PASS CRITERIA: 100% of calls > $0.01 cost or high-risk category (e.g., document-reading, email-sending) carry a signed receipt. Signature algorithm is Ed25519 (not HMAC-SHA256, which is symmetric and doesn't prove auditor key custody).
EVIDENCE: 10 sample receipts (DSSE format), signature verification with auditor's public key, canonical JSON diff proving no mutation
MAPS TO: EU AI Act §12 (tamper-resistant logging), NIST SP 800-161 (supply-chain risk: integrity), in-toto ITE-6 + SLSA (Provenance attestation)
SEVERITY: CRITICAL — unsigned logs can be forged by the vendor post-facto; signature is the proof buyers want
AG-A3 | Signature Key Custody (Auditor-Held)

TEST: Verify the Ed25519 signing key is NOT stored in customer's codebase. Confirm it's held offline (src/auditor-attestation.js#loadAuditorKeyFromFile pattern: separate env var KOLM_AUDITOR_ED25519_PRIVATE_KEY_PATH or HSM). Show key fingerprint (first 32 hex of sha256(SPKI)). Confirm key_fingerprint is hard-coded in verifier so a different key cannot be swapped in.

PASS CRITERIA: Auditor's private key never appears in customer's git/docker/CI logs. Public key is published on a tamper-evident host (website TLS cert, GitHub releases, OPAQUE public-key directory). Key rotation is documented (overlap_until field from keys.js).
EVIDENCE: auditor's public key + fingerprint, key publication proof (screenshots + TLS chain), key rotation records, HSM or encrypted-file custody statement
MAPS TO: SOC2 CC6.3 (privileged access management), NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 (key life cycle), in-toto (key transparency)
SEVERITY: CRITICAL — key custody is the trust anchor; if customer holds the signing key, attestation is worthless
AG-A4 | Call-Level Trace Capture (W3C Trace Context)

TEST: For a representative call (e.g., agent processes a document), extract the trace_id, span_id, parent_span_id, and payload. Verify trace follows W3C Trace Context spec (32-hex trace_id, 16-hex span_id). Verify payload includes: kind (llm_call / tool_call / io / state / etc.), model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency_ms, cost_usd, redaction_applied, error_code (if any).
PASS CRITERIA: >=95% of calls include a structured span with all required fields. Traces are queryable (can retrieve a span by trace_id in <1s from database).
EVIDENCE: sample trace spans (JSONL format), schema definition, query latency proof
MAPS TO: EU AI Act §12 (automatic logging), NIST RMF (monitoring logs), OpenTelemetry standard (W3C Trace Context binding)
SEVERITY: HIGH — without structured traces, call attribution and cost recovery are guesswork
AG-A5 | Input/Output Hashing (Content-Addressed)

TEST: For the same representative call, verify input_hash and output_hash are sha256(content) with optional truncation to 16+ hex for grep-ability (src/gateway-receipt.js pattern: 'sha256:<16-64hex>'). Verify hash is deterministic (same input → same hash, always). Verify hashes are embedded in signed receipt so they cannot be tampered with post-hoc.
PASS CRITERIA: All calls >= high-risk category carry input_hash and output_hash. Hashes are sha256, not md5. Hashes are included in signed receipt's canonical JSON.
EVIDENCE: receipt snippet showing hashes, hash verification (re-compute sha256 and compare), determinism test (same input run twice)
MAPS TO: NIST SP 800-14 (integrity), content-addressable storage (IPFS principle), EU AI Act §12 (audit trail content)
SEVERITY: MEDIUM — hashing proves exact input/output, not just that a call happened
AG-A6 | Redaction Tracking (PII Holdout)

TEST: Identify calls that might contain PII (emails, phone numbers, SSNs, etc.) and verify redaction mode. For each redaction_applied field in receipt (from src/pii-redactor.js), verify it records: class (email, phone, ssn, etc.), count, and mode (detect_only / redact_captures / redact_all / block). Verify PII-redacted content is NOT included in audit log (only redaction_applied metadata).

PASS CRITERIA: For data containing regulated PII, redaction_applied is recorded for every call. Zero unredacted PII in audit logs. Redaction library is deterministic (same text → same redaction positions).
EVIDENCE: 5-10 sample receipt snippets showing redaction_applied field, PII-detector library version, audit log sample verifying PII absence
MAPS TO: GDPR Article 32 (security), California SB-833 (AI security baseline), EU AI Act §12 (PII redaction implied in 'automatic logging')
SEVERITY: HIGH — unredacted PII in logs is a data breach; also violates GDPR
AG-A7 | Retention Compliance (6-Month Cliff Aug 2 2026)

TEST: Query retention policy: 'how long are logs kept?' Verify answer is >=6 months. Verify there is a documented retention schedule (e.g., 'keep raw calls 6 months, aggregated metrics 2 years'). For EU-regulated customers, verify Aug 2 2026 date is in roadmap.
PASS CRITERIA: Logs are retained >=6 months from call date. Policy is in written form (handbook, SLA, ToS). Deletion is automated (not manual) with audit trail.
EVIDENCE: data retention policy document, deletion automation code/screenshots, dated commit showing 6-month-plus commitment
MAPS TO: EU AI Act §12 (automatic, tamper-resistant logging; 6-month retention; enforcement cliff Aug 2 2026), GDPR Article 5(1)(e) (storage limitation)
SEVERITY: CRITICAL (for EU customers) — Article 12 violation risks fines up to €30M or 6% revenue
AG-A8 | Verifiable Audit Log Extraction (Buyer Audit)

TEST: Emulate the buyer's audit: export 3 months of logs for a specific agent + tenant + date range. Verify export includes all required fields, is reproducible (same query → same export), and comes with a signature proof (from auditor's key). Verify the export can be verified offline (e.g., buyer can run `kolm verify --attestation <export.json> --key <auditor-pubkey-pem>`).

PASS CRITERIA: Log export is JSON or JSONL with stable schema. Export includes a signature block (src/auditor-attestation.js pattern). Verifier tool exists and produces 'signature OK' or 'FAIL' message.
EVIDENCE: sample export file, verifier tool output, offline verification (no internet required)
MAPS TO: SOC2 CC7.4 (event log review), EU AI Act §12 (audit trail accessible to buyers), NIST CP-11 (contingency planning; auditable exports)
SEVERITY: HIGH — if buyer cannot extract and verify logs, the attestation is hollow
PROMPT INJECTION & JAILBREAK RESISTANCE (5 controls) — Answers: Has the agent been tested against known attacks? Are guardrails in place? Is the scope clear (tested, not warranted)?
AG-R1 | Known Attack Categories Tested (OWASP LLM Top 10 / ATLAS)

TEST: Run a red-team checklist against the agent. For each of OWASP LLM Top 10 (Injection, Data Exfiltration, Training Data Poisoning, etc.) + MITRE ATLAS v5.4 (prompt injection, memory manipulation, context confusion), document: (a) the attack is in scope, (b) a test case was run, (c) the result (blocked / detected / passed-through), (d) if passed-through, the root cause. Severity: if injection can exfiltrate secrets or cause unintended actions, mark as CRITICAL.

PASS CRITERIA: >=5 of the 10 OWASP LLM categories are tested (injection, exfiltration, poisoning, model denial-of-service, supply-chain, plugin-design, insecure output, vector-db poisoning, excessive agency, model theft). Each has a documented test case + result. Critical findings are remediated.

EVIDENCE: red-team checklist (signed by tester), test cases (prompts + expected/actual output), remediation records, disclaimer ('tested for injection resistance, not warranted; new attack patterns may emerge')
MAPS TO: OWASP LLM Top 10, MITRE ATLAS v5.4 (4.1 Prompt Injection, 4.2 System Prompt Leakage, 5.1 Memory Poisoning), NIST AI RMF (GV-3 monitoring for drift in model behavior)
SEVERITY: CRITICAL — injection is the #1 attack surface for agents; if untested, buyer will not close
AG-R2 | Guardrail Implementation (Input Filter + Output Validation)

TEST: Identify any input filters (e.g., regex blocks on SQL keywords, prompt-injection keywords). For each filter, verify: (a) it's applied before the LLM sees the input, (b) it has a clear bypass/allowlist policy, (c) it's logged (so we know when it fires). Similarly, verify output validation: (a) the agent's response is parsed for safety (e.g., JSON schema validation for tool calls), (b) unsafe outputs are rejected or flagged.

PASS CRITERIA: >=1 input filter is in production. Output validation exists (not just pass-through). Filters are logged (visible in audit trail). Filter bypass requires explicit approval (not ambient).
EVIDENCE: guardrail code, filter configuration, sample fire logs, bypass approval process
MAPS TO: OWASP ASI-01 (Excessive Agency, guardrails), OWASP LLM Top 10 (Prompt Injection countermeasures), NIST RMF (detect and react controls)
SEVERITY: HIGH — guardrails alone don't stop injection, but they raise the bar and provide audit trail
AG-R3 | Jailbreak Test Catalog (Known Patterns)

TEST: Run the agent against a curated set of known jailbreak prompts (e.g., 'you are now in developer mode', 'ignore previous instructions', role-play prompts, etc.). Document pass/fail. If any pass, escalate to critical.
PASS CRITERIA: Agent rejects or ignores >80% of known jailbreak patterns. Any jailbreak success is documented + remediated. New jailbreak patterns are tracked (e.g., monthly re-test).
EVIDENCE: jailbreak test case list (signed by tester), test results (pass/fail/detection), remediation records, re-test cadence
MAPS TO: OWASP LLM Top 10 (Prompt Injection), MITRE ATLAS (Prompt Injection), EU AI Act §15 (testing and validation)
SEVERITY: CRITICAL — if agent can be jailbroken, all permission controls are bypassed
AG-R4 | Red-Team Engagement (3rd Party, Optional)
TEST: (Optional upgrade path) Hire a red-teamer to run a half-day attack against the agent. Document findings. Offer as premium tier ($15K-$25K add-on).

PASS CRITERIA: Red-team engagement occurred in last 90 days. Report is signed by tester. High-severity findings are remediated. Report includes a 'limitations' section (e.g., 'testing did not cover adversarial-example poisoning of training data').
EVIDENCE: red-team engagement contract, signed report, remediation records, limitations statement
MAPS TO: NIST RMF (risk assessment), ISO 42001 §9.1 (monitoring and measurement), SOC2 Risk Assessment component
SEVERITY: MEDIUM (optional) — buyers pay for this when stakes are high ($500K+ deal)
AG-R5 | Scope & Limitations Statement (Critical Disclaimer)

TEST: Verify the attestation includes a clear scope paragraph: 'Attestation covers: permission audit, audit-trail integrity, known-attack testing. Attestation does NOT cover: model-intrinsic robustness (e.g., can the model itself be tricked?), training-data poisoning, novel attack patterns, hardware-level attacks, supply-chain integrity beyond Kolm'.
PASS CRITERIA: Scope & Limitations section is >=200 words, signed by auditor, included in final report.
EVIDENCE: attestation report excerpt (Scope & Limitations section)
MAPS TO: Legal risk management, NIST RMF (risk framing), SOC2 (scope and limitations of management assertion)
SEVERITY: CRITICAL — missing scope kills buyer confidence and creates liability

OPERATIONAL & DATA GOVERNANCE (4 controls) — Answers: Is the customer running agents in a controlled environment? Can they version-control and roll back agents? Is there a security incident response plan?
AG-O1 | Environment Isolation (Staging vs. Production)

TEST: Verify staging environment exists and is separate from production (different API keys, databases, secret stores). Verify agents can be tested in staging before production deploy. Verify there is a deploy approval gate (e.g., PR review, manual approval).
PASS CRITERIA: Staging exists. Every production deploy requires human approval (no auto-deploy). Rollback procedure exists and is tested.
EVIDENCE: staging environment description, deploy approval policy, rollback procedure, recent deploy records
MAPS TO: SOC2 CC7.1 (environment segregation), NIST SP 800-53 (CM-2 baseline configuration), ISO 42001 §8.2 (change management)
SEVERITY: MEDIUM — weak environment isolation enables accidental production changes
AG-O2 | Agent Version Control & Reproducibility

TEST: For a production agent, verify: (a) the agent's definition (system prompt, tools list, parameters) is in git (or equivalent), (b) the agent can be reconstructed from git + dependencies, (c) the agent's version hash is tied to the audit trail (so we know which version was running when a call was made).

PASS CRITERIA: Agent definition is in git with blame/history. Agent version is recorded in every call's audit trace (payload.agent_version or similar). Reproducibility test: git checkout old version, re-run agent, verify output matches recorded trace.
EVIDENCE: git log of agent config, version field in sample trace, reproducibility test results
MAPS TO: ISO 42001 §8.2 (change management), NIST RMF (configuration management), in-toto + SLSA (provenance traceability)
SEVERITY: MEDIUM — version ambiguity breaks audit trail correlation
AG-O3 | Incident Response Procedure (Security Incidents, Not Just Operational)

TEST: Query: 'if an agent was compromised or made an unintended call, what's the response plan?' Verify answer includes: (a) who to notify (incident commander), (b) how to extract affected calls from audit log, (c) procedure to revoke credentials, (d) timeline to notify customers (if data exfiltration). Verify the plan is written and tested (tabletop exercise in last 6 months).
PASS CRITERIA: IR plan exists and is documented. Plan includes an audit-log extraction step. Tabletop or drill was run in last 6 months. Plan is tested to completion (not just drafted).
EVIDENCE: IR plan document, tabletop exercise notes, timeline/decision records
MAPS TO: SOC2 CC7.3 (incident response), NIST SP 800-61 (incident handling), ISO 42001 §8.4 (security incident management)
SEVERITY: MEDIUM — if you don't know how to respond to a compromise, you're exposed
AG-O4 | Data Governance & Consent Tracking (Regulated Data)

TEST: If the agent processes personal data (PII, health data, etc.), verify: (a) a consent record exists for each data subject (e.g., 'user agreed to AI processing on 2026-01-15'), (b) the consent is recorded in the audit trail, (c) there is a data deletion mechanism (GDPR right-to-be-forgotten). If the agent processes regulated data categories (health, financial, EU resident), verify DPA or BAA is in place.

PASS CRITERIA: For EU-regulated data: DPA signed. Consent records are queryable. Deletion is automated and audited. For US HIPAA data: BAA signed. For all regulated data: audit trail shows when data entered/exited the system.
EVIDENCE: DPA/BAA copy, consent record schema, deletion audit trail, data classification policy
MAPS TO: GDPR Article 5-7 (lawfulness, consent, data minimization), HIPAA §164.504(e) (BAA requirements), EU AI Act §12 (consent + logging for high-risk)
SEVERITY: CRITICAL (conditional on regulated data) — missing DPA is a showstopper for EU enterprise deals
SUMMARY SCORING:
Pass all 24 controls → READY (agent passes enterprise review)
Pass 20-23 controls → READY WITH REMEDIATION (specific gaps listed, timeline to fix)
Pass 15-19 controls → AT RISK (multiple critical gaps, buyer unlikely to close without 2-4 week fixes)
Pass <15 controls → NOT READY (advise customer to table deal 4-6 weeks, fix major gaps)
ATTESTATION REPORT SPECIFICATION (What the buyer receives)
FILE FORMAT: JSON with embedded Ed25519 signature (in-toto DSSE + kolm-audit-v1 extensions)
FILENAME PATTERN: <customer>-agentic-security-attestation-<date>-signed.json
SIZE: ~50-100 KB uncompressed
DELIVERY: PDF + JSON (PDF is human-readable, JSON is machine-verifiable)
JSON STRUCTURE (canonical, sorted keys, signed):
// Envelope (in-toto DSSE standard)
"payloadType": "application/vnd.in-toto+json",
"payload": "<base64url-encoded JSON>",  // payload decoded below
"signatures": [
"keyid": "auditor-key-fingerprint",
"sig": "<base64url Ed25519 signature>"
// Decoded payload (readable, not base64):
_payload_decoded: {
// === METADATA ===
"spec_version": "agentic-security-attestation-v1",
"attestation_id": "asa_20260607_<unique>",
"auditor": {
"org": "Kolmogorov Audit",
"auditor_id": "auditor_name / email",
"key_fingerprint": "abc123def456...",  // first 32 hex of sha256(pubkey SPKI)
"public_key": "-----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----\n...",  // Ed25519 SPKI PEM
"accreditation": "SOC 2 Type II 2025 / ISO 27001:2022" (optional)
"customer": {
"name": "Acme AI",
"contact": "security@acmeai.com",
"attestation_date": "2026-06-07",  // ISO 8601
"valid_until": "2026-09-07",  // 90-day re-attestation window
// === EXECUTIVE SUMMARY ===
"summary": {
"overall_status": "READY" | "READY_WITH_REMEDIATION" | "AT_RISK" | "NOT_READY",
"readiness_score": 22,  // out of 24
"critical_issues": 0,
"high_issues": 2,
"medium_issues": 0,
"headline": "Agent passes enterprise security review on permission and audit controls; two high-risk findings require remediation within 30 days.",
// === CONTROL FINDINGS (24 controls, each with assessment) ===
"findings": [
"control_id": "AG-P1",
"control_name": "Agent Inventory Complete",
"category": "Permission & Delegation",
"status": "PASS" | "PASS_WITH_NOTES" | "FAIL" | "NOT_TESTED",
"severity": "CRITICAL" | "HIGH" | "MEDIUM" | "LOW",
"description": "Verified that customer maintains an automated agent registry covering 98% of production agents, updated within 24 hours of deployment.",
"evidence": [
"Screenshot of /v1/agents endpoint (2026-06-06T14:22:00Z)",

PRICING & ENGAGEMENT MODEL:
Fixed Fee: $25,000 per 2-4 week engagement (includes on-site/remote audit + report + 1 remediation re-test)
Contingency: 5-10% of deal value if closed within 60 days (requires customer to notify auditor)
Retainer: $2.5-5K/month (quarterly re-attestation, log monitoring for drift, 48h-response incident support)
Premium Tier: +$15K-25K for 3rd-party red-team engagement (PyRIT/Garak/custom, per-model injection tuning)
SLA:

decisions: DECISION 1: 24 Controls (not 40+) — Ruthless prioritization. Tests ONLY what blocks enterprise deals: permission audit + audit-trail integrity + known-attack resistance + operational governance. Excluded: model quality benchmarks, cost optimization, feature completeness. Rationale: buyers cite SOC2/ISO/NIST as blockers, not model MMLU scores.

DECISION 2: Ed25519 Signing + in-toto DSSE (not HMAC / JWT / custom scheme) — Reuses existing kolm infrastructure (auditor-attestation.js, intoto-receipt.js, keys.js). Auditor key is separate from customer key (keys.js#loadAuditorKeyFromFile, env var KOLM_AUDITOR_ED25519_PRIVATE_KEY_PATH). Signature is over canonical JSON (deterministic sort) so verifier cannot be tricked by key-ordering attacks. Baseline: 3-10% attestations fail signature verification due to mutation during CI/delivery; in-toto DSSE prevents all of those.

DECISION 3: JSON + PDF (not DOCX / HTML / custom) — JSON is machine-verifiable (buyers can write `jq` scripts to extract control statuses). PDF is human-readable (lawyers, CISOs, CFOs review it). PDFs with embedded Ed25519 fingerprints prevent post-hoc tampering via screenshot.

DECISION 4: Audit trail must include PER-CALL signatures, not just retention policy — SOC2/ISO/NIST auditors ask 'prove a log entry wasn't altered.' Retention policy alone ('we keep logs 6 months') is soft evidence. Per-call signed receipts (from src/gateway-receipt.js + src/intoto-receipt.js) are hard evidence. Buyers' auditors will verify 10 random signatures; 1 failure = fail.

DECISION 5: 90-day attestation validity (not perpetual) — EU AI Act §12 enforcement cliff Aug 2 2026 means regulations shift. Perpetual attestations become liability ('we attested on 2026-01-01 but EU rules changed on Aug 2'). 90-day window forces re-test cadence, catches drift, and keeps remediation current. Retainer model ($2.5-5K/mo) funds this.

DECISION 6: Scope EXCLUDES prompt-injection defense (tested, not warranted) — Red-teaming AG-R1 covers OWASP LLM Top 10 / MITRE ATLAS known patterns. But new jailbreaks emerge weekly. Attestation scope statement (AG-R5) explicitly disclaims 'novel attack patterns.' Liability is tight: 'we tested for injection resistance against known patterns; new attacks may emerge.' Customers can buy premium red-team add-on ($15K-25K) if they want novel-attack coverage.

DECISION 7: Control AG-A2 (per-call signatures) is CRITICAL, not aspirational — AWS/GCP/Azure all require signed logs for compliance. Enterprise RFPs cite 'tamper-evident logging.' If customer doesn't have per-call signatures, fail AG-A2 and recommend immediate remediation (2-3 day eng effort). This is the #1 thing that unblocks deals.

DECISION 8: Buyer talking points (one page, 7 bullets) embedded in report — Enterprise security reviews are committee meetings (CISO, CTO, Risk, Procurement, Legal). They need a 1-page summary they can screenshot and email to each other. Placing it in the JSON attestation means it travels with the signature.

DECISION 9: Contingency fee (5-10% of deal value) requires customer to report close within 60 days — Without this gate, disputes are inevitable ('was the attestation really the reason we closed?'). Clear window (60 days) and required notification (customer emails auditor) creates a clean record. Contract language: 'Contingency is earned when customer notifies auditor of deal close in writing within 60 calendar days of attestation signature date.'

DECISION 10: Premium red-team tier ($15K-25K, PyRIT/Garak) is positioned as optional — Not all deals require it. $100K ACV deals (small customers) won't pay. $500K ACV deals will. Offer as 'if your buyer is asking for novel-attack coverage, we have a 3-day red-team engagement for $20K.' Separates nice-to-have from must-have.

DECISION 11: Re-attestation after remediation is fast + cheap (3-5 days, $3K-5K) — Customer fixes AG-P2 (API keys). Auditor re-runs AG-P2 + spot-checks AG-A1 (log integrity) + spot-checks AG-R1 (injection). NOT a full re-audit. This is the retainer model: $3-5K per delta, payable from contingency fee or retainer.

DECISION 12: Auditor's public key is published on a TLS-protected website + embedded in attestation — Buyer's auditor will verify two things: (a) signature is valid, (b) signer's key is not forged. Publishing on kolmogorov.ai/audit/keys/<fingerprint>.pub with TLS cert proves ownership. Embedding key in JSON attestation lets verifier fall back to offline verification if website is unavailable.

DECISION 13: Control AG-O4 (data governance) is CRITICAL for EU/regulated data, MEDIUM for US — DPA/BAA are deal-blockers for $500K+ EU deals. For US mid-market, they're nice-to-have. Verdict: separate the control into two: AG-O4a (data governance, MEDIUM severity baseline) + AG-O4b (DPA/BAA, CRITICAL only if EU data). Simplifies scoring.

DECISION 14: Evidence for each control is minimal (3-5 concrete items, not a 20-page audit trail) — Attestation is for deal-unblocking, not regulatory filing. Each control has <=5 evidence items: screenshots, code snippets, audit-log exports, test results. Full audit-workpaper trail lives in auditor's files, not in the delivered report.

DECISION 15: Attestation report is SIGNED ONCE at delivery, not continuously — Temptation: 'let's re-sign the report every time auditor updates a finding.' That breaks chains-of-custody. Rule: attestation is signed once at delivery. If findings change, increment the attestation_id and re-sign a new report. Buyer keeps both; verifier can compare.
open_questions: Q1: Should attestation include a machine-readable control status API endpoint (e.g., GET /v1/audit/status/agentic/asa_20260607_xxx) so buyers can automate acceptance? Or is JSON file + manual verification sufficient? Trade-off: endpoint enables real-time compliance check (buyer's dashboard shows 'SOC2-ready: yes/no') but adds operational burden (auditor must maintain a signing service). Counter: retainer model already includes this (quarterly re-test).

Q2: Red-teaming (AG-R1 through AG-R4) — Should auditor run red-team or require customer to hire external tester? Reuses (PyRIT/Garak/custom prompt injection framework)? Current design: auditor runs known-pattern testing (AG-R1/R2/R3 = baseline); premium tier ($15K-25K) = hire 3rd-party red-teamer. But some customers may not have budget; should baseline include a more intensive red-team?

Q3: What is the auditor's reputational / liability exposure if an agent passes attestation and then gets compromised 4 months later? Current disclaimer (AG-R5, Scope & Limitations) reads 'tested for injection resistance against known patterns; new attacks may emerge.' Is that sufficient legal cover? Recommend legal review of attestation contract + liability cap.

Q4: Should attestation include a 'compliance drift detection' service (e.g., 'monitor the customer's logs for changes to agent permissions; alert if drift detected')? That would be a retainer add-on, maybe +$1-2K/mo. Upside: catches silent permission creep, re-remediates AG-P7. Downside: auditor becomes an embedded compliance officer. Scope creep?

Q5: Contingency fee (5-10% of deal value) — Should auditor track which attestations actually contributed to deal closure? (Requires customer to send NDA'd deal records.) Or just accept customer's word ('we closed $250K because of your attestation, so you get 5%')? Recommend: ask customer to state deal value + close date in attestation-signed-off email; use that as basis for contingency. Document it in a simple contract.

Q6: ISO 42001 (AI Management System) — This is a new, still-evolving standard (published 2024, revisions expected). Should attestation include a version pin ('ISO 42001:2024 edition') or just cite it generically? Recommend: version the attestation spec to ISO edition (agentic-security-attestation-v1-iso42001-2024). Avoids ambiguity.

Q7: MCP (Model Context Protocol) supply-chain audit — Product brief mentions 'MCP supply-chain audit (492 unauthenticated servers, 1,184 malicious skills) as an adjacent product.' Should baseline attestation include MCP-specific controls (e.g., 'verify all MCP servers are authenticated' or 'MCP OAuth 2.1 + PKCE scoping'), or is that a separate offering? Recommendation: separate offering; baseline is agent-scoped, not MCP-scoped.

Q8: Should attestation support a 'lightweight fast-track' mode for smaller deals (e.g., <$100K ACV)? Fast-track: 5 critical controls only (AG-P1, AG-P2, AG-A1, AG-A2, AG-R1) in 1 week for $8K. Full attestation: 24 controls in 2-4 weeks for $25K. Rationale: land fast, upsell full audit later if customer grows. Trade-off: two different reports confuses market positioning. Recommendation: start with one package ($25K, 24 controls); offer fast-track only on request.

Q9: Buyer acceptance of third-party attestation — Hypothesis in product brief is that enterprise buyers will accept a kolm-signed attestation if it maps to SOC2/ISO/NIST. Reality check: most Fortune 500 security teams still require HITRUST or Big-4 auditor. Should attestation marketing pivot to 'evidence for your own SOC2 audit' (auditor's input, not a replacement)? Or double down on 'kolm is SOC2-ready' messaging? Recommendation: A/B test both; measure win rates.

Q10: Retainer SLA (quarterly re-attestation, $2.5-5K/mo) — What happens if customer doesn't apply remediation within 90 days (e.g., AG-P2 wildcard keys)? Does attestation auto-expire? Or does auditor re-issue with a degraded status ('READY_WITH_CRITICAL_REMEDIATION_OVERDUE')? Recommendation: contract language: attestation is valid 90 days; if customer pays retainer, auditor does quarterly re-test and updates status. If remediation is not applied, status downgrades to AT_RISK.

design.component: Agent Security Review Readiness Audit + Signed Attestation — ICP, Positioning, Packaging & Pricing
design.summary: Crystallized a sharp ICP (Series A AI-native startups, 20-60 engineers, selling agentic products into enterprise, stuck in security review), positioned against Okta/Vorlon/Vanta as deal-unblocking (not tooling), packaged as fixed-fee audit + signed report + contingency fee + retainer, and priced for $25K/mo cash velocity via 5 design partners in 8 weeks. Mapped every finding to SOC2/ISO 42001/NIST/EU AI Act/OWASP, leveraged founder's Ed25519 signing + trace capture + PII redaction + append-only audit trail as the production IP spine.
details: ICP FIRMOGRAPHICS & TRIGGER: Series A AI-native startups (typical raise $30-75M), 12-24 months old, 20-60 engineers, founder-led with no dedicated CPO/security FTE. Economic buyer = technical founder or CTO. Trigger event = ONE enterprise deal worth $100K-$500K ACV is STALLED (not lost) in a 4-8 week security review. Pain is quarter-end revenue slip, internal champion political capital burn, competitor wins, headroom toward Series B tightens. Addressable cohort: ~100-150 AI-native startups actively selling agentic products into enterprise; founder's warm network is exactly this group. Design-partner conversion target: 5 founders at ~$8K/mo in 8 weeks = $40K/mo committed revenue (path to $25K/mo fixed-fee + contingency + retainer math).

POSITIONING STATEMENT (vs competitors): NOT a compliance vendor (unlike Okta/Vorlon/Vanta). Sell as 'deal unlocker'— expedited (2-4 week delivery), fixed-fee AI-agent security review that ends in a cryptographically SIGNED (Ed25519) attestation report the founder hands DIRECTLY to the enterprise buyer's security review, compressing it from 4-8 weeks back to 5-10 days and recovering the quarter. Differentiating triplet: (1) least-privilege permission audit (enumerate agents/delegations/scoped vs wildcard keys — prove Scope 1-2 readiness), (2) red-team/prompt-injection proof (PyRIT/Garak automation tuned per model), (3) tamper-evident signed report (Ed25519 custody + in-toto envelope) mapped to FRAMEWORKS THE BUYER ALREADY CITES (SOC2 CC1-CC9 for agents, ISO 42001, NIST AI RMF AG-GV/AG-MP/AG-MS/AG-MG, EU AI Act Article 12 tamper-resistant logging, OWASP ASI01-ASI10, CSA AICM, MAESTRO). Undercuts HITRUST 50-70% (HITRUST ~$35K-$100K / 6-18mo; this is ~$25K fixed / 2-4 weeks) and beats timeline ~10x.

PACKAGING: Three components, one motion. (A) FIXED-FEE AUDIT ($25K, 2-4 week delivery): on-site or remote assessment of agent inventory (enumerate all agents, tools, API keys, delegations, data flows), permission posture (baseline vs least-privilege), audit/logging maturity (append-only capture, redaction, retention), and red-team results (prompt injection, context poisoning, system-prompt leakage tested against models in production). Output = detailed findings doc + signed attestation report (PDF + JSON envelope) the founder shows the buyer. (B) CONTINGENCY FEE (5-10% of deal value, capped at deal close within 60-90 days post-attestation): ties success to outcome — founder is price-insensitive if it works ($25K to unblock $250K+). Must be watertight contract tying fee to deal close within defined window + before/after tracking. (C) RETAINER ($2.5K-$5K/mo): monthly re-attestation + remediation tracking + delta audit (new agents, permission changes, compliance updates). Covers re-signing when founder adds agents, rotates keys, or regulatory baseline shifts (Colorado AI Act June 30, California SB-833 July 1, EU AI Act Article 12 ENFORCEMENT CLIFF Aug 2 2026). Sustains customer relationship for next round funding diligence + expanded agentic surface.

PRICING RATIONALE & $25K/MO MATH: Fixed fee ($25K) is anchored to three factors: (1) Competitive undercut = HITRUST baseline cost / 2-3, (2) Marginal auditor cost = ~$8K-$12K labor (senior AppSec engineer, 80-120 hours at $100-150/hr + tooling) once permission-analysis framework is templated, (3) Founder willingness to pay = psychologically price-insensitive above $15K when a $250K deal hangs (empirical from YC SaaS playbook). Contingency (5-10%, mean 7.5%) is NOT a sure revenue item — only counts if deal closes AND attestation was material to outcome. Conservative model: assume 40% of audits result in a deal close within window; 60% of those would have closed anyway (attestation marginal utility = 67% of the closing cohort).
