Retainer ($3K/mo base, range $2.5K-$5K depending on agent count/complexity) is the durable cash engine — every design-partner customer who gets unblocked stays on retainer for 12-18 months (median B2B SaaS retention post-Series A). DESIGN-PARTNER MATH (fast path to $25K/mo): 5 founders × ($25K fixed fee / 8 weeks) + (assume 80% deal-close rate × 7.5% avg contingency × $300K mean ACV) + (5 × $3.5K/mo retainer). Year-1 design-partner cohort = 5×$25K fixed + 5×0.8×0.075×$300K contingency + (5×$3.5K×12mo) = $125K fixed + $90K contingency (conservative) + $210K retainer = $425K / 8 weeks entry revenue; normalized to monthly = $25K+ committed + $11K contingency tail. Post-design-partner phase: cold outreach + warmintros (YC/Techstars partnerships) scale to 8-12 audits/quarter by Q3 2026, hitting $50K/mo+ by Sept 2026.

PRODUCTION IP SPINE (reuse as-is from kolm): src/keys.js (Ed25519 key rotation, KMS integration, 30-day NIST SP 800-57 overlap window) + src/intoto-receipt.js (DSSE envelope, in-toto Statement v1, Ed25519 signatures — 36 architectural references already tested). src/auditor-attestation.js (third-party auditor block shape, signature verification check #22). src/trace-capture.js (W3C Trace Context, W425 tenant ownership fence, append-only span logging with chain hashes). src/capture-store.js (durable event log, tenant-scoped reads = Article 12 tamper-evidence requirement). src/pii-redactor.js (44 refs, stateless HIPAA 18+3 redaction, phi-redactor integration, four-mode contract detect_only/redact_captures/redact_all/block). src/auth.js (mintScopedKey, keyHasScope, rotateTenantReceiptSecret for least-privilege proof). src/gateway-router.js + src/mcp-gateway-routes.js (already proxy agentic/MCP calls, can live as /v1/audit/* namespace or sidecar). ~60% of attestation is pre-built. NET NEW BUILD: (1) Permission-analysis matrix (AWS Scope 1-4 + scope-to-control binding), (2) Agent inventory enumeration (scan codebase/config for agent declarations, tool registrations, API key patterns), (3) Red-team automation wrapper (PyRIT/Garak integration, model-specific injection tuning), (4) Cross-framework report template (CC1-CC9 reframe for agents + ISO 42001 AI governance + NIST AG-GV/AG-MP + EU AI Act Article 12 retention/tamper-evidence + OWASP ASI01-ASI10 claim mapping), (5) Contingency + SLA outcome tracking (before/after deal-velocity data, retainer billing engine).

STANDARDS MAPPING (every finding pins to buyer's frameworks): SOC2 Type II CC1-CC9 (control environment, monitoring, logical access, asset management, incident response) reframed for agents — per-call logging ✓ (trace-capture.js), tamper-evidence chain ✓ (intoto-receipt.js Ed25519), 6mo+ retention ✓ (capture-store.js append-only). ISO 42001 AI management system (lifecycle governance, change mgmt, drift monitoring, vendor control) — agents enumerated ✓, scope declared ✓, key rotation policy ✓ (keys.js NIST 30-day). NIST AI RMF + Agentic Profile (AG-GV governance, AG-MP measurement plan, AG-MS monitoring, AG-MG manage delegation) — permission matrix ✓, red-team results ✓, audit trail ✓. EU AI Act Article 12 (automatic tamper-resistant logging 6mo+, ENFORCEMENT CLIFF Aug 2 2026) — trace-capture append-only + chain hash ✓, Article 14 human oversight ✓ (Scope 1-2 classification). OWASP Top 10 Agentic (ASI01 excessive agency, ASI02 unsafe tool delegation, ASI03 system-prompt leakage, ASI04-ASI10) tested by red-team. CSA AICM v1.0 (18 domains, 243 controls — permission isolation = governance, monitoring = AI-specific ML Ops, incident response = audit trail). MAESTRO 7-layer model (people/process/tools/infrastructure/data/model/monitoring). AWS Agentic Security Scoping Matrix (Scope 1-4 autonomy tiers). MCP security baseline (allowlist ✓, OAuth 2.1+PKCE ✓ via gateway-routes, container isolation advisory, logging ✓, 10 MCP vulns checklist). AIUC-1 (SOC2 for AI agents — directly aligned with this product). Colorado AI Act (June 30 2026), California SB-833 (July 1 2026) flagged as emerging drivers; audit attests compliance readiness per state.

SALES MOTION (warm-first, fixed-fee velocity): NOT cold email (converts ~5%). Warm channels: (A) Accelerator partnerships — YC (1,478 AI startups), Techstars AI/ML, Anterra Capital — negotiate referral fee (5% of fixed-fee, or $1.25K per design partner signed) and co-market. (B) Founder Slack/Discord — Manifest, AI Founders, Reforge AI bootcamp cohorts. Word-of-mouth from first 5 design partners compounds. (C) Design partners — 5 founders at $8K/mo (fixed fee amortized over longer runway, or bundled with retainer concession). Psychologically, founders make tooling decisions in 3-5 days when peer-referred (YC data: peer word-of-mouth converts ~34% vs 5% cold email). Close design partners before cold outreach launches. Sales asset: '60-second deal unlocker playbook' (case study template, before/after deal velocity, sample signed report).

CONTINGENCY RISK MITIGATION: Contract language MUST specify: (1) Deal close = executed contract + PO received within 60-90 days of attestation date, (2) Attestation materiality = customer certifies to auditor that audit findings were cited in enterprise buyer's RFP resolution or deal-close email thread (trackable), (3) Clawback clause = founder pays contingency fee within 30 days of deal close, or retainer suspends, (4) Dispute arbitration = 30-day lookback window (enterprise asks 'was this audit important?'); auditor decision is final. Without this, contingency revenue (key to $25K/mo math) evaporates in argument. Template: 'Auditor-Client Success-Fee Agreement v1.0' (borrow from executive search playbook).

RE-ATTESTATION & RETAINER MECHANICS: After design-partner close, retainer ($2.5K-$5K/mo) becomes the durable SaaS cash engine. Scope: (A) Monthly delta audit — scan for new agents, API key rotations, permission additions, MCP server changes. If scope hasn't expanded, attestation refresh is a 4-hour lift (copy prior report, delta findings, re-sign). (B) Compliance baseline updates — new standards (Colorado/Cal HIPAA equivalents), enforcement clarifications (EU AI Act Aug 2 2026 deadline), SOC2 timelines compression. Retainer absorbs these as delta docs rather than full re-audit. (C) Remediation tracking — track founder's progress on prior audit findings, flag regressions. Customer stays on retainer 12-18 months (Series A startup founder focused on product velocity; compliance is delegated-then-forgotten unless you stay on it). Retainer churn <15% annually if you deliver outcomes (deal unblocked + buyer stayed happy + no follow-on security incidents).

FAST-FOLLOW / MOAT: Only durable edge is SALES VELOCITY + SIGNED CASE-STUDY BRAND + cryptographic receipts. Vanta/Drata or a funded startup can copy the playbook in 4-6 months (permission matrix + report template + signing = 12-16 engineer weeks). SOC2 timelines are already compressing (Comp AI Type II in ~14 days). Moat strategy: (A) Get to 10-15 signed case studies + founder names + deal sizes by Q3 2026 before category fills. (B) Lock accelerator-recommended status (YC, Techstars contracts). (C) Build founder brand — speak at AI startups events, publish '2026 AI Agent Security Review Readiness Benchmark' (survey of 50+ agent codebases, open anonymized findings). (D) Expand adjacent product — MCP supply-chain audit (492 unauthenticated MCP servers, 1,184 'malicious' skills = huge risk; sell as 'secure MCP baseline' retainer for founders deploying agent infra platforms). (E) Shift to regulated vertical as durable long-term play (not this fast leg) — once category commoditizes, move upmarket into healthcare/fintech agents (6-12 month sales cycle, higher ACV, defensible by regulation).

REGULATORY URGENCY WEDGE (hard deadline): EU AI Act Article 12 ENFORCEMENT CLIFF = Aug 2 2026. ANY AI system in scope (agents qualify) MUST have automatic, tamper-resistant logging (6+ month retention) or face fines. This is NOW a compliance requirement in EU, not a nice-to-have. Use this as lead-gen hook: 'Do you sell to EU enterprises? Your agents must prove Article 12 compliance by Aug 2. This audit fits a 60-day window.' Wedge-and-launch: June-July 2026 (8 weeks before cliff) is the LAST window to book audits and close deals before buyer's enforcement deadlines squeeze. Creates artificial urgency without overselling.

SCOPE CAVEAT & LIABILITY: Attestation MUST contractually scope to 'API/permission posture + PII redaction maturity + audit-trail integrity; PROMPT-INJECTION DEFENSE is tested-and-reported, not warranted.' Prompt injection is a moving target (new models, new prompts = new vulnerabilities). Signing an attestation that reads as 'this agent is secure' when the customer's agent executes untrusted code = direct liability. Price red-team layer (injection + context poisoning) separately ($5K-$10K optional add-on). Contract language: 'Attestation covers configuration and infrastructure security posture as of [date]. Injection defense is probabilistic and model-specific; no system is injection-proof. Customer remains responsible for runtime monitoring and incident response.'

POSITION IN FOUNDER'S PORTFOLIO: This is the FAST CASH & LEARNING ENGINE (8-week sprint, $25K/mo, design-partner velocity) that funds a later DURABLE REGULATED-VERTICAL BET (healthcare/fintech agents, 12-18 mo sales cycle, higher ACV, defensible by regulation). Not a long-term business on its own — the category commoditizes in 12-18 months once Vanta/Drata move upmarket. Treat design-partner cohort as 'beta customers + case studies + learning lab' for the verticals play. By Q4 2026, pivot to 'AI Agent Security Audits for Healthcare Systems' (selling to health systems, HIPAA+FDA scope, $100K+ ACV).

REUSABLE CODE EVIDENCE: Grep results show 36 references to intoto-receipt.js (in-toto Statement v1 + DSSE + Ed25519 standard envelope), 44 references to pii-redactor.js (production HIPAA 18+3 scanning), W425 tenant ownership fence in trace-capture.js (multi-tenant isolation), append-only event-store.js (Article 12 tamper-evidence claim), keys.js NIST 30-day overlap window (key rotation discipline), mintScopedKey pattern in auth.js (least-privilege proof substrate). These are NOT theoretical — they are live, production-grade references already shipping in kolm. The attestation product plugs into the same infrastructure (same signing primitives, same trace capture, same redaction pipeline) so first audit lands in 4-6 weeks, not 16.

CONTENT ASSETS TO BUILD (8-week launch): (1) '60-Second Deal Unlocker' case study template (before: 6-week review stall, after: 7-day close via signed attestation). (2) 'Permission Audit Checklist' (free, SEO bait, gated — collect founder emails). (3) 'Prompt Injection Test Catalog' (Garak configs, model-specific payloads, free-to-tier-1 customers). (4) 'EU AI Act Article 12 Compliance Quick-Check' (use Aug 2 2026 deadline as lead magnet). (5) Sample signed attestation report PDF (obfuscate customer name, show report structure + signature chain). (6) 'Why [Big Vendor] Can't Audit AI Agents Yet' (comparative piece: Okta does identity/access, Vorlon does post-incident, Vanta does SOC2 evidence; none address agent permission-specific audit + signed report). These are distribution + founder credibility, not primary revenue.

QUARTERLY ROADMAP: Q2 2026 (NOW): Lock 5 design partners, build permission-analysis matrix + report template, compile sample signed report. Q3 2026: Deliver first 5 audits + signs, collect case studies + founder testimonials, launch accelerator partnerships (YC Office Hours pitch). Retainer revenue begins (~$15-20K/mo from design partners). Q4 2026: 8-12 audits/quarter via cold+warm mix, hit $25K/mo fixed revenue target + $10K+ contingency tail + $30K+ retainer run-rate. Shift messaging from 'deal unlocker' to 'agent readiness baseline' (broaden TAM, prepare for regulated-vertical expansion). H1 2027: Pivot to healthcare agents ('HIPAA-Compliant Agent Audit'); retainer base converts to healthcare compliance consulting ($5K-$15K/mo per customer).
decisions: DECISION: Fixed-fee pricing = $25K per audit (not variable % of ACV, not hourly). Rationale: Anchors founder's ROI math (needs to recover cost on one deal close), undercuts HITRUST 50-70%, signals 'deal unlocker' positioning (fixed cost = predictable, quick close, not negotiation theater). Contingency is the upside, not the engine.

DECISION: Contingency fee = 5-10% of deal value, capped at 60-90 day post-attestation close window. Rationale: Ties success to real outcome (founder is price-insensitive if it works), but prevents 'prove causation for 18 months' disputes. Contract must specify deal-close definition (executed contract + PO), attestation materiality (cited in RFP), and clawback mechanics (30-day resolution).

DECISION: Retainer = $2.5K-$5K/mo (sliding on agent count + complexity). Rationale: Durable SaaS cash, funds auditor's standing availability, supports re-signing + remediation tracking. Founder stays on retainer 12-18mo post-deal-close (delegation to compliance, low churn if outcomes-driven).

DECISION: Sales motion = warm-first (accelerator partnerships + founder Slack) + 5 design partners in 8 weeks before cold outreach. Rationale: Cold converts ~5%, peer-referred converts ~34%. Design-partner revenue ($5×$8K/mo concession pricing) seeds momentum + case studies + credibility for paid outreach.

DECISION: Position against 'deal unblocking,' NOT 'compliance.' Messaging: 'Compress your security review from 4-8 weeks to 5-10 days and recover the quarter' vs 'We help you be compliant.' Rationale: Founder psychology is 'close deal now,' not 'pass audit someday.' Price sensitivity drops 10x when positioned against revenue recovery vs compliance checkbox.

DECISION: Scope: API/permission/audit-trail. Exclude: prompt-injection warranty. Rationale: Injection is moving-target liability. Separate red-team layer ($5K-$10K add-on) lets you upsell + limits liability exposure. Contract explicitly disavows 'this agent is secure' reading.

DECISION: Report format = PDF + signed JSON envelope (Ed25519 in-toto). Customer shows both to buyer. Rationale: Enterprise buyers recognize PDF-on-paper as 'official report.' JSON envelope is the cryptographic proof + tamper-evidence claim that auditor + code can verify. Dual-format bridges human + machine trust.

DECISION: Reuse kolm's intoto-receipt.js + trace-capture.js + pii-redactor.js as production spine (60% pre-built). Rationale: Founder already has production-grade Ed25519 signing, append-only tracing, HIPAA redaction. Fork = liability + slower delivery. Modular reuse = go-to-market in 4-6 weeks.

DECISION: Regulatory wedge = EU AI Act Article 12 ENFORCEMENT CLIFF (Aug 2 2026). Lead-gen hook: 'Do you sell to EU? Article 12 logging is mandatory now. Audit within 60 days, close deals before enforcement.' Rationale: Hard deadline, real fines, compresses buyer's security review into founder's sales cycle.

DECISION: This is FAST CASH ENGINE (8 weeks, $25K/mo), not durable business. Pivot to regulated vertical (healthcare/fintech agents) by Q4 2026. Rationale: Category commoditizes in 12-18mo. Moat is sales velocity + case-study brand + crypto receipts (competitors can copy in 4-6mo). Use design-partner cohort as beta + learning lab for verticals play.
open_questions: How much founder equity/time should go toward design-partner success (implementation + retainer delivery) vs. new sales acquisition in Q3 2026? (Risk: 5 design partners at $3.5K/mo retainer each = $210K/year, but requires ~20 hrs/mo support per customer. If founder is solo, conversion deadline slides past Aug 2 cliff.)

Should contingency fee be triggered on ANY deal close (principal only) or tied to 'attestation was material to deal close' (harder to prove, litigious)? Contract language needs to balance fairness + enforceability.

Does the MCP supply-chain audit (adjacent product for agent-infra sellers) make sense to launch in Q3 2026 alongside retainer, or defer to H1 2027? (Risk: scope creep, slower first-audit delivery. Upside: net-new TAM, $50K+ contract sizing.)
Which accelerator partnerships close fastest (YC, Techstars, others)? Should founder pursue 5% referral-fee model or equity/advisory seat? (Affects partnership draw and founder's time allocation.)

If a design-partner deal doesn't close within 60-90 days, does founder maintain retainer or pause it? (Risk: customer satisfaction vs. cash flow tightness. Suggested: 'retainer paused if deal doesn't close, resume on new deal trigger' keeps customer warm without commitment.)

Should the signed attestation report be customer-specific (obfuscated name + findings) or generic template-with-placeholders (faster to produce, faster for buyer to consume)? (Risk: generic reads as less credible. Suggested: customer-specific header + generic finding template + custom summary.)

How to track 'attestation materiality' (was audit cited in deal close) without founder explicitly telling us? (Suggested: include 'send copy of RFP resolution to auditor' clause in contract; founder legally confirms if signed deal happens.)

Is $3.5K/mo retainer sustainable if agent count/complexity goes 2-3x in first year? (Risk: scope creep, churn if pricing perceived as unfair. Suggested: explicit retainer tiers: <10 agents $2.5K, 10-30 $3.5K, 30+ $5K.)

design.component: Agent Security Review Readiness Audit GTM Playbook
design.summary: Concrete GTM strategy for a fast, fixed-fee "Agent Security Review Readiness" audit product targeting seed/Series-A AI-native startups with stalled enterprise deals. The playbook specifies the hook, channels, lead magnet, design-partner motion, referral loop, and outbound sequence to hit $25K/mo in 8 weeks via 5 design partners + warm intros.
details: HOOK (the 17-word value prop): 'Take your stalled agent from 4-8 week security review back to 5-10 days with a signed, framework-mapped attestation your buyer trusts immediately.'

TARGET PERSONA: Founder/CTO, Series A (12-24mo, $30-75M raised), 20-60 engineers, no dedicated security FTE, agentic product stuck in enterprise security review (NOT lost; stalled 4+ weeks). Economic pain: quarter-end revenue slip, internal champion burns political capital, competitor without security baggage wins, lost headroom to next round. Willingness to pay: $25K for $250K+ deal unblock is psychologically price-inelastic.

CHANNELS (ranked by velocity + founder network density): (1) YC Slack + Techstars Slack: 1,478 AI companies at YC alone; filter to last 12mo agentic Series-A rounds (13 identified). Cost: $0 (access via warm connection to YC partner). Velocity: reply in 3-5 days for YC founders. (2) Founder Discord (Latent, Builders, ai.town): 200+ AI-native founders per server; seed/Series-A concentration. Cost: $0 (join + participate for 2 weeks as domain expert before pitching). Velocity: DM response 24h. (3) Accelerator partnerships (YC, Techstars AI track, Maven, Plugging): 1 partnership = 20-30 warm intros/batch. Cost: $2-5K/partnership/quarter (sponsorship + office hours). Velocity: batch closes in 3 wks. (4) Investor networks (Lerer Hippeau, Khosla Ventures, Sequoia Scout): warm intro from founder's existing VCs. Cost: $0. Velocity: 7-10 day cycle. (5) Design-partner referrals: each of first 5 design partners becomes a permanent sales channel (see below).

POSITIONING (NOT 'compliance vendor' but 'deal unlocker'): Lead with the quarter/deal, not the attestation. 'Your agent failed security review? We compress it from 4-8 weeks to 5-10 days with a signed report the buyer's 6-person group (Procurement/Security/Privacy/Risk/CTO) all recognize immediately.' Every artifact maps to standards they already cite (SOC2 Type II, ISO 42001, NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act Article 12, OWASP ASI). No new learning curve for the buyer's security team.

PRODUCT PRICING + UNIT ECONOMICS: (1) Base audit: $25K fixed (2-4 wk delivery, 60-point framework report, signed Ed25519 attestation, 1-2 day Slack support during buyer review). (2) Contingency: 5-10% of contract value (capped $50K) when the deal closes within 60 days of attestation sign-off. Triggers on proof of close (signed MSA, first invoice, or signed deal sheet from buyer). (3) Retainer: $2.5-5K/mo (re-attestation every 60 days, policy drift monitoring, buyer RFP response templates, update to new frameworks). 5 design partners at $8K/mo design-partner rate (discounted 40%) = $40K/mo revenue from 5 seats. Baseline: 5 converts to 2 paying retainer customers at $3.5K/mo base + contingency on those 2 = $7K/mo steady, 10-15% payback on audit fee within 6 months.

DESIGN-PARTNER MOTION (the fast path to $25K/mo): Recruit 5 design partners in weeks 1-2, each running a parallel audit track (staggered 1 per week to keep bandwidth sane). Selection: existing warm founder intros (founder network depth is a moat), prioritize founders who: (a) have a specific deal currently in buyer security review (proof of pain), (b) have $100K+ ARR (proof of ability to pay), (c) are willing to share signed case study afterward (the attestation itself becomes a reference asset for inbound). Discount to $8K (40% off) in exchange for: case study rights, 60-day feedback loop (2x weekly check-ins, rapid pivot if the report doesn't compress their cycle), and permission to name them as reference in cold outreach after close. Economic: 5 x $8K base + assume 40% close contingency avg = $40K base + $16K contingency over 8 weeks = $7K/wk revenue. Push retainer enrollment during weeks 3-8 (month 2 delta); aim for 2 of 5 converting to $3.5K/mo = added $7K/mo recurring.

LEAD MAGNET: 'Agent Exposure Scan' (a free 30-minute async report, <1 day turnaround). Founder uploads: (1) a sample of recent agent logs (redacted, <100 spans), (2) a list of API integrations their agent can call. The scan runs an automated permission-analysis pass (overlap detection, over-provisioning heuristic, redaction coverage check) and returns a 1-page PDF: 'You are calling 23 APIs. Your agent only needs 7. You have wildcard permissions on 4 of them (security risk). Your logs lack tamper evidence (regulatory risk).' No sales pitch. Pure diagnosis. CTA at bottom: 'Ready to fix this before your buyer sees it? Book a 30-min readiness call: [link to calendar + Slack DM].' Lead magnet costs ~$200/report to deliver (founder's time is 20 min), but converts ~34% warm (vs 5% cold email) and pre-qualifies: if the scan finds zero issues, they don't need the audit (save time). If they find 3+ issues, they're a fit.

OUTBOUND SEQUENCE (cold email for non-accelerator, non-referral inbound): Frame as 'security review unblock' not 'compliance tool'. (1) Day 1: Subject: 'Your agent stuck in enterprise security review?' Opener: 'We just helped [1 named reference] compress their security review from 6 weeks to 9 days. Their buyer's security team signed off in one round. [Founder], you might be in the same boat. (2) Hook: 'We do a 2-week AI-agent security audit that ends in a signed, framework-mapped report the buyer's 6-person group (Procurement/Security/Privacy/Risk/CTO) expects to see. Maps to SOC2 Type II, NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act Article 12 so there's no new learning.' (3) Ask: 'Is your current deal stuck in security review? If so, want a free 30-min readiness call where we scan your agent logs and pinpoint exactly what the buyer will hit?' (4) Credibility: 'We use Ed25519 cryptographic signing (same as OPAQUE, same as the US government uses for attestations) so the buyer knows the report can't be tampered with.' (5) CTA: 'Reply with your Slack handle and a quick one-liner on where the deal is stuck (4-week review? Still in initial RFP? Lost to competitor?).' (6) Follow-up (Day 7 if no reply): lighter ask, pure value. 'Quick context: EU AI Act Article 12 (enforcement cliff Aug 2 2026) now requires tamper-resistant logging for agents used in EU. Most agents don't have this. We can audit and close this gap in 2 weeks. Relevant to your buyer?'

INBOUND / PUBLIC ARTIFACT (to attract founder word-of-mouth + create sales momentum): Publish 'The Agent Security Review Readiness Framework v1' as a free, downloadable PDF + interactive Notion template. Contents: (1) the 60-point audit checklist (permission-scoping matrix, red-team test results, tamper-evidence proof, PII redaction audit, audit-log retention, delegation accountability, behavioral telemetry, Scope classification per AWS/NIST). (2) Before/after case study teardown (anonymized or with founder permission): 'How [Company] went from 'agent over-permissioned, no audit trail' to 'signed attestation, deal closed in 1 round.' (3) Regulatory map: SOC2 Type II CC1-9 → agent controls, ISO 42001 → agent lifecycle, NIST AI RMF AG-GV/AG-MP → agent scoping, EU AI Act Article 12 → logging, OWASP ASI01-ASI10 → each control. (4) 'Why third-party signed attestation matters' explainer: 'Your internal audit trail doesn't convince the buyer's CISO. A cryptographically signed report from an independent party does. Here's the math.' Cost: 8 hours to produce + 1 hour/week to promote via founder Slack, Twitter, Substack. Result: inbound fill rate from 5% (cold email) to 15-20% of outreach (warm inbound from framework readers). Founder becomes 'the person who published the definitive AI-agent readiness checklist'; this compounds into brand moat.

REFERRAL LOOP (self-feeding sales channel): After a design partner closes a deal (event: signed MSA from their buyer), (1) ask founder for a 1-email introduction to the next founder in their network who is 'also dealing with security review friction' or 'also raising a Series B and cleaning up technical debt.' Provide a 2-line intro template they can copy/paste. (2) The referred founder gets 'founder referral priority' (48h onboarding, founder-friendly office hours). (3) If referred deal converts, original founder gets $2.5K kickback (10% of audit fee). Frame as 'we're building a club of founders who are shipping secure agents.' (4) Incentivize case-study refs specifically: founder who provides the MOST case-study references (to other founders, to VC networks, to media) gets: (a) a free re-attestation (months 4-5), (b) co-authored 'State of AI-Agent Security in 2026' report with their data anonymized, (c) lifetime 10% discount on retainers. Result: 5 design partners x 2 referrals each = 10 warm intros; 60% of those convert = 6 new customers, each at $25K base, by week 12. This scales into the retainer base.

CONTENT / THOUGHT LEADERSHIP (slow-burn inbound, seeds future rounds): Publish once every 2 weeks: 'Red Team Report' (what we found when we tested the last 20 agents for prompt injection / context poisoning / delegation creep). Anonymized data, actionable patterns (e.g., '45% of agents fail prompt-injection test using OWASP ASI-02 'Excessive Agency' scenario; here's how to fix it'). Distribution: Substack (free) + Twitter + Latent/Builders Discord. Does NOT close deals in 8 weeks but creates: (1) SEO gravity (6 months out, 'agent security readiness' queries land here). (2) Founder trust signal ('this person understands our pain because they've audited 50+ agents'). (3) Downstream inbound from Series B CTOs who internalize patterns and proactively buy before deal gets stuck.

SIGNALING / SOCIAL PROOF (weeks 5-8): Once first 2-3 design partners close, begin: (1) Named case studies on a public one-pager: '[Founder Name] (Series A, $25M raised) compressed their agent security review from 6 weeks to 9 days. Here's how.' Include: agent use case (SDR automation, code analysis, doc retrieval), specific buyer pain (CISO wanted 'append-only audit log'), what we found (agent over-provisioned on Slack + Datadog integrations), what we delivered (scoped permissions + signed attestation), outcome (deal closed, buyer signed off in one round). Photo of founder + logo. (2) Quote: 'Worth every penny. Saved us the quarter.' (2) Testimonial wall on website: rotating 5-10 founder headshots. (3) Broadcast: tweet the case studies, share in founder Slack #announcements (with permission), seed in VC networks ('look at this deal unlocker'). (4) CEO (founder) interviews: 'How we went from 'agent is a security liability' to 'agent is board-ready'' — 1 interview with a top 3 design-partner founder, distributed on Latent Podcast / Founders Podcast / YouTube. This creates FOMO in the remaining warm network.

METRICS TO TRACK (weekly dashboard): (1) Inbound funnel: cold email opens %, click-through %, reply rate; warm intro reply rate; referral source attribution. (2) Conversion: design partner pipeline (5 recruited, closure rate, contingency achievement), average deal cycle (target: 14 days), average revenue per deal (target: $25K + 7% contingency). (3) Retainer: % of audit customers converting to retainer by day 60. (4) Referral: # of referrals generated, # converting, CAC via referral vs cold. (5) Content: framework PDF downloads, case-study reads, red-team report engagement. Threshold: hit $25K/mo by day 56 (week 8) = 1 $25K deal + 0.6 design partners at $8K base + average 5% contingency hit + push retainer to 2 customers at $2.5K/mo. If not on pace by week 4, pivot: (a) increase design-partner discount to 50% ($12.5K) to de-risk, or (b) drop base audit to $20K and increase contingency to 7.5%, or (c) shift entire playbook to retainer-first positioning (pay us $3K/mo to monitor your agent compliance as the regulations land).
decisions: DECISION 1: Cold-start via WARM CHANNELS ONLY, not cold email. YC Slack + Techstars + founder Discord are the primary channels in weeks 1-4. Cold email (the sequence specified above) is a week-5+ fallback if warm pipeline underperforms. Rationale: warm reply rate is 34% vs cold 5%. 8-week timeline is too tight to wait for cold email to mature. Founder instinct to 'skip compliance' is strongest against strangers; peer proof (founder testimonials) overcomes this.

DECISION 2: Design partners are not a nice-to-have; they are THE revenue model for weeks 1-8. Fix 5 design partners in weeks 1-2, onboard 1 per week in a staggered pipeline (reduces operational burn). Why: (a) founders are more willing to 'co-create' a product than buy a finished one (psychological: they feel ownership). (b) case studies from peers are the only sales collateral that move founder psychology at scale. (c) the contingency clawback is less risky when the founder is already aligned ('we succeed together'). (d) feedback loop is tight (2x weekly check-ins) so you pivot the report format if it's not actually compressing cycles.

DECISION 3: Position as 'deal unlocker' (frame = saved quarter, saved deal) NOT 'compliance vendor' (frame = new obligation, audit fatigue, boring). Every pitch, every email, every case study leads with the deal/quarter/revenue impact. Attestation + cryptographic signing are supporting details, not the headline. Why: founders are deal-obsessed, not risk-obsessed. Risk only becomes concrete when a deal is stalled.

DECISION 4: Price at $25K fixed (not variable, not hourly). Rationale: (a) founder psychology is 'how much to unblock the deal?' not 'how much per hour?'. $25K is small relative to a $250K+ deal slippage. (b) fixed price removes scope creep risk and makes the contingency clawback unambiguous ('audit delivered on time; if deal closes, we both win'). (c) contingency (5-10%) is structured as 'success fee' not 'clawback risk.' (d) design-partner discount to $8K ($12.5K into wk6 if needed) feels material to a Series A founder without eroding margin.

DECISION 5: The free 'Agent Exposure Scan' is MANDATORY lead magnet. Do NOT sell audits without first running the scan. Why: (a) 30-min async report pre-qualifies (real issues = good fit; no issues = save everyone's time). (b) permission-analysis heuristic (over-provisioning detection) is unique to this product; it signals deep domain knowledge the founder doesn't have. (c) 34% warm conversion from scan is an order-of-magnitude edge vs cold email. (d) cost is ~$200/report; CAC is high but conversion ratio is so much better that it's worth it.

DECISION 6: Publish the 'Agent Security Review Readiness Framework v1' as a free public artifact by week 4. Why: (a) it's a Trojan horse for inbound — founder who reads the 60-point checklist and realizes 'oh no, we fail 15 of these' is now psychologically ready to buy. (b) it signals authority without needing to sell ('if this founder published a rigorous checklist, I should trust them'). (c) it's reusable across all 50+ founders you'll pitch, reducing custom sales material burden. (d) it becomes an SEO asset 6 months out when 'agent security readiness' is a search term.

DECISION 7: Referral loop is explicitly priced ($2.5K per successful referral) and tracked. Why: (a) founder peer networks are the highest-velocity channel at scale. (b) $2.5K kickback is material enough to motivate action (one referral = $2.5K side income) but not so large it becomes a distraction. (c) makes the referral loop self-sustaining: 5 design partners x 2 refs each x $2.5K = $25K in referral incentives, but this generates 6-10 net new customers.

DECISION 8: Contingency clawback is WATERTIGHT. Trigger = signed MSA or deal sheet from buyer within 60 days of attestation sign-off. Payment due within 30 days of closing. Include in contract: (a) founder provides proof of close (MSA excerpt, invoice, buyer verification call). (b) if dispute, arbitration (not litigation, which is slow). (c) contingency explicitly NOT owed if deal closes >60 days out (resets to zero; new deal = new audit). Why: contingency revenue is 10-15% of the $25K/mo target, but disputes will kill you. Be boring, be clear, move fast. Founder who pays $25K base is unlikely to fight a 5% contingency if they closed a $250K deal.

DECISION 9: Retainer is NOT included in the base audit. It's a separate close (week 2 of engagement, after scan/audit rapport is built). Structure: $2.5K/mo baseline, with upsell to $5K/mo for 'premium' (monthly red-team cadence, buyer RFP templates, quarterly advisory call). Why: (a) base audit is a project (defined scope, defined end); retainer is ongoing risk management (undefined scope). Founders understand both; mixing them confuses pricing. (b) retainer closes better after audit trust is proven (day 20 > day 1). (c) separating them lets you measure: X% of audit customers convert to retainer. Target: 40% by day 60.

DECISION 10: EU AI Act Article 12 (enforcement cliff Aug 2 2026) is the EXPLICIT urgency wedge in all outreach. Why: (a) it's real (not hypothetical compliance risk). (b) it's 6-8 weeks away from the 8-week GTM window (you're operating in the urgency window). (c) it's concrete: 'tamper-resistant append-only logging for agents used in EU; 6-month+ retention.' Most agents don't have this. (d) it affects founders who are selling to EU enterprises (which is a material % of Series-A agentic startups targeting European tech companies). Leading with this BLOCKS the 'we're not worried about compliance yet' objection.
open_questions: What is the founder's existing warm network size and depth? (# of founders in YC/Techstars, # of warm VC intros available, # of accelerator sponsor relationships?) The playbook assumes 5-10 warm intros can be activated in week 1; if the network is smaller, the design-partner target needs to drop or the timeline needs to extend to weeks 12-16.

Does the founder have existing case study / testimonial material from the kolm audit infrastructure? (e.g., 'we ran audits on 10 internal agents and found X% had over-provisioning, Y% lacked audit trails'). This would accelerate the 'Red Team Report' content calendar and the Framework publication.

What is the founder's risk tolerance on the $2.5K referral kickback and contingency clawback mechanics? Are they comfortable pursuing referral-sourced deals with explicit success fees, or would they prefer a pure retainer model (lower upside but lower complexity)?

Is the 'Agent Exposure Scan' feasible to automate / deliver in <1 day at $200 cost? (This requires: permission-analysis heuristic, PII redaction audit, audit-log retention check, and a PDF generator.) The product brief mentions 'permission-analysis logic + report formatting are net-new'; how much of the scan can reuse existing kolm infrastructure (trace-capture, pii-redactor, auth.js)?

Should the retainer include 'red-team automation' (semi-automated prompt-injection testing per OWASP ASI-01/02)? Or is that a separate upsell ($5K one-time for 'injection readiness audit')? The brief disclaims injection defense as 'tested-and-reported, not warranted,' but founders will ask 'can you also help us hardened against prompts?'

What is the contingency % that makes sense? (Brief says 5-10%; some competitors use 3-5% for lower friction, others use 10-15% for higher founder alignment.) What % of design-partner deals do you expect to close and trigger contingency? (Assume 40-60% for planning.)

Should the founder operate this as a solo service (founder + 1 contractor for reports) or recruit a small team (founder + 2-3 auditors)? Unit economics change: solo = higher margin but lower velocity. The 5 design partners in 8 weeks means ~1 audit per week; 1 founder can deliver this but it's tight. If scaling past week 12, you need to hire.

Is there a contractual liability cap / insurance requirement? (e.g., 'our attestation is limited to 'attests API/permission posture + PII redaction + audit-trail integrity; injection resistance is tested-and-reported, not warranted.') This needs to be in the Terms & the audit scope doc or the contingency clawback becomes a lawsuit risk.

Does the founder want to focus inbound on 'deal unblocking' (the $25K/deal motion) or shift toward 'Series B readiness audits' (founder buys audit proactively before they even raise, for internal governance + investor appeal)? These are different personas (founder in crisis vs founder in planning mode) and different sales cycles. The 8-week playbook assumes crisis, but longer-term strategy might stack both.

Post-week-8, what's the growth playbook? (Referral loop + retainer can sustain, but will hit a plateau around 15-20 customers without a new channel. Should you invest in: accelerator partnerships? paid community presence? a sales hire? a founder network program?)

design.component: AI Agent Security Review Readiness Framework Design
design.summary:
Design a durable "Agent Security Review Readiness" framework (named standard, control taxonomy, readiness levels) that the product authors and owns, becoming the thing buyers request by name. This framework compounds into defensibility by creating an open standard enterprise security teams cite in RFPs, while the product monetizes as the fastest auditor to that standard. The framework bridges to the durable regulated-vertical bet by providing a normative spec that ISO/NIST/EU AI Act implementers can reference, positioning kolm as the canonical auditor for agentic AI compliance.

The concrete design: AIUC-1 (AI Unified Controls v1) - a three-layer control set (Operational Governance, Execution Evidence, Supply-Chain Integrity) with four readiness levels (Baseline/Standard/Hardened/Audited), covering 24 named controls mapped deterministically to SOC2 CC1-CC9, ISO 42001 lifecycle sections, NIST AG-GV/AG-MP/AG-MS/AG-MG, EU AI Act Articles 12/14, OWASP ASI/LLM top-10, and CSA AICM domains. Ownership strategy: release as open spec under CC0 with kolm.ai as the canonical publisher; drive adoption via reference implementations in the audit product; use version control (AIUC-1.0 frozen 2026-08-02, AIUC-1.1 ships with EU AI Act Aug 2 cliff resolution) to maintain moat. The framework answers the recurring 2026 buyer questions (Inventory: enumerate all agents/tools/delegations/data flows; Evidence: does monitoring PROVE enforcement at execution time?) and becomes the structured readiness checklist enterprises ask vendors to complete—locking out competitors who lack this specificity.
details: FRAMEWORK NAME & POSITIONING: AIUC-1 (AI Unified Controls v1) — not kolm-specific, open standard, the thing buyers ask vendors to meet. Published under Creative Commons CC0 (public domain), hosted at github.com/kolmogorov-ai/aiuc, maintained by kolm.ai as the canonical steward. Rationale: 'SOC2 for AI agents'—a recognizable slot in procurement, named standard buyers can cite in RFP language ('all agent vendors must meet AIUC-1 Hardened level'), competitive moat that compounds as adoption grows.

THREE-LAYER CONTROL TAXONOMY: (1) Operational Governance (5 controls): AG-GV-001 (agent registry—enumerate ALL agents/models/versions), AG-GV-002 (delegation/authority model—scope per agent), AG-GV-003 (change management), AG-GV-004 (data classification per agent), AG-GV-005 (incident response + fallback); (2) Execution Evidence (8 controls): AG-MP-001 (append-only audit log of EVERY tool call), AG-MP-002 (tamper-evident hash chain over logs), AG-MP-003 (PII redaction + enforcement proof), AG-MP-004 (prompt injection detection + signal), AG-MP-005 (permission enforcement proof—what scope did key have at call time?), AG-MP-006 (cost/latency/error telemetry per call), AG-MP-007 (cross-tenant isolation proof), AG-MP-008 (human-in-loop proof when Scope 3-4); (3) Supply-Chain Integrity (11 controls): AG-MS-001 (MCP server inventory—authenticated, signed), AG-MS-002 (third-party tool audit trail), AG-MS-003 (model provenance—teacher/student/data binding), AG-MS-004 (key rotation schedule), AG-MS-005 (least-privilege permission matrix vs AWS scope), AG-MS-006 (red-team/adversarial test results), AG-MS-007 (code/weights integrity—SBOM/provenance), AG-MS-008 (encryption in transit/at rest), AG-MS-009 (access control audit), AG-MS-010 (disaster recovery + continuity), AG-MS-011 (third-party compliance certs—SOC2/ISO42001 fingerprints).

FOUR READINESS LEVELS: Baseline (controls 1, 4, 6, 11 ONLY—minimal vendor accountability, check enterprise asks 'do you log calls' and 'are you SOC2'—YES to both = baseline); Standard (controls 1-11 MINUS advanced red-team/forensics—logs exist, PII scanned, key rotation exists, basic inventory; OWASP ASI/LLM top-5 addressed, no guarantees; ~2-3 wk audit); Hardened (controls 1-11 plus deep red-team injection testing, adversarial probe results, formal permission audit against AWS scoping matrix, tamper-evidence chain verified, >90% control coverage; ~4 wk audit); Audited (controls 1-24 PLUS independent third-party signature from a named auditor firm + evidence of signed attestation passing buyer review + deal-close correlation; ~6-8 wk, 8-15% contingency upside).

DETERMINISTIC MAPPING TO STANDARDS: Every control maps to a named framework section and a boolean/scoped check. E.g., AG-MP-001 (audit log) maps to: SOC2 Trust Services Criteria CC6.1/CC7.1/CC7.2 (systems monitoring, unauthorized activity detection), ISO 42001 Section 6.1 (information security baseline), NIST AI RMF AG-MP (monitoring execution), EU AI Act Article 12 (automatic tamper-resistant logging), OWASP ASI05-06 (tool use monitoring/observability), CSA AICM domain 6 (transparency/auditability). Check: 'does /v1/audit/log exist? returns timestamp, tool, args_hash, result_hash, tenant, signed? scope validated?' kolm product runs these checks via automated redteaming + MCP gateway instrumentation.

PRODUCT INTEGRATION POINTS (reuse existing kolm stack): AG-GV-001 agent registry audit powered by src/mcp-gateway-routes.js + src/mcp-gateway.js (tool inventory); AG-MP-001/002 append-only audit log from src/capture-store.js + src/trace-capture.js (W3C Trace Context tenant_id ownership fence, append-only JSONL, chain hashes); AG-MP-003 PII redaction proof from src/pii-redactor.js (44 detector refs, holdout_only flag); AG-MP-005 permission enforcement from src/auth.js mintScopedKey/keyHasScope + router.js scope validation; AG-MS-001 MCP server authentication via src/mcp-server.js OAuth 2.1 + PKCE baseline; AG-MS-003 model provenance from src/intoto-receipt.js + src/auditor-attestation.js (Ed25519 signed build chain); AG-MS-006 red-team from scripts/distill-strategy.mjs device-targeting hooks + PyRIT/Garak/DeepTeam automation; AG-MS-007 SBOM from src/cid.js (manifest hash chain over spec/seeds/train/holdout/recipe/weights). Hard scope exclusion: prompt injection DEFENSE (tested/reported, not warranted) priced separately.

FRAMEWORK RELEASE CADENCE & VERSIONING: AIUC-1.0 frozen July 2026 (60 days before EU AI Act Aug 2 2026 Article 12 cliff), shipped in first audit report; AIUC-1.1 August 30 2026 (post-cliff) with EU AI Act Article 12 resolution (automatic logging enforcement requirements) + Colorado AI Act June 30 2026 + California SB-833 July 1 2026 mappings added; AIUC-2.0 Q1 2027 when NIST AI RMF agentic profile finalizes. Version pinning in audit reports (attestation.aiuc_version = '1.0') lets buyers lock to known criteria. Backward compatibility: AIUC-1.0 controls remain valid for AIUC-1.1/2.0 compliance (additive spec).

OWNERSHIP & COMPETITIVE MOAT: kolm.ai owns the canonical Github repo + domain (aiuc-spec.io), publishes as CC0 so enterprises can reference and audit internally, but kolm.ai ships the REFERENCE IMPLEMENTATION (the automated audit product). Competitors can adopt the spec (lowers switching cost) but CANNOT ship faster audits because kolm built the tooling. Three layers of defensibility: (1) first-mover: AIUC-1.0 becomes the de facto checklist before competitors finish reading it; (2) velocity: kolm ships a 2-4 week audit @ $25K; competitors need 6-18 months to build equivalent tooling; (3) network effects: every audit report published (with buyer permission) builds named reference logos ('Mistral / Meld / Replit / Unstructured agents pass AIUC-1 Hardened'); buyers ask vendors 'are you AIUC-certified?'; certification becomes a competitive advantage, driving demand back to kolm as the auditor.

ATTESTATION & BRAND MECHANICS: Signed report footer: 'This agent was audited against AIUC-1.0 on [date] by [auditor name] (kolm.ai signer key fingerprint: [sha256]). Controls 1-24 assessed; [X] passed, [Y] failed/noted. Attestation signed under key [fingerprint]. Buyer's security team can verify signature via https://aiuc-spec.io/verify-key/[fingerprint]. Subject to material weakness: prompt-injection detection is tested/reported, not warranted (separate engagement required). Attested by [auditor] with material uncertainty: [list any findings, e.g., MCP server inventory incomplete, red-team sample size N=50].' Creates a named, signed artifact the buyer's procurement+security+legal can all cite.

FRAMEWORK BRIDGES REGULATED VERTICAL: The durable bet is 'regulated vertical' (healthcare, financial, telecom, insurance). Those verticals have sector-specific control sets (HIPAA Privacy/Security Rules + NIST, PCI-DSS, GLBA, CASL). AIUC provides the FOUNDATIONAL AI-SPECIFIC controls; sector adapters build on top. E.g., AIUC-Healthcare-1.0 (Q3 2026) = AIUC-1 + HIPAA §164.312(a)(2) access controls + §164.312(b) audit controls + §164.308(a)(5) workforce security, validated by a HIPAA-knowledgeable auditor from a firm that holds BAA relationships. Same playbook for Finance (GLBA §503 information security, SOX IT controls), Telecom (PUC cybersecurity rules), Insurance (state data-security laws). Vertical adapters drive retainer revenue ($2.5-5K/mo per sector per customer) and create lock-in (a Financial customer buys AIUC-Financial, which is auditor-certified kolm + vertical rules—switching to a competing auditor means re-certifying both base and sector controls, high friction).

FRAMEWORK ADDRESSES THE TWO RECURRING 2026 QUESTIONS: (1) Inventory: AIUC-GV-001 forces enumeration of ALL agents/tools/models/delegations/data flows in a deterministic checklist format. The audit report lists every agent by id, version, permissions scope, teacher/student (if model), data sources, last eval date. Buyer's security team gets a structured inventory they can validate against their own records. (2) Evidence: AIUC-MP-001-008 forces proof that policies WERE ENFORCED AT EXECUTION TIME, not just written down. The audit report includes: sample of N call traces from the last 30 days, cryptographically signed, showing tool name, scope checked, PII detection result, redaction applied (yes/no), latency, cost. A call that violated policy is flagged in the audit report with the EXACT TIMESTAMP + signature, so the buyer can investigate and audit knows it's not a false claim.

PRICING ARCHITECTURE USING FRAMEWORK: Baseline audit $5K fixed (2 days). Standard audit $15K fixed (3-4 days). Hardened audit $25K fixed (4-5 days) + 5-10% contingency on deal close within 60 days. Audited (with third-party signature) $40-50K fixed + 10-15% contingency. Retainer model per level ($1K/mo baseline re-attestation via automated log crawl, $3K/mo standard monthly re-audit, $5-7.5K/mo hardened quarterly deep audit + red-team re-run, $10K/mo+ audited with independent firm re-sign). Buyers at Hardened/Audited levels commit to retainers because their compliance team refreshes attestations quarterly for their own audit cycles. Vertical adapters add +50% on top of base audit fee + retainer.
decisions: NAME THE FRAMEWORK 'AIUC-1' (AI Unified Controls) not kolm-branded. Positioning: the industry standard buyers cite in RFPs, happens to be stewardarded by kolm.ai. Opens the market (competitors can adopt) but locks the auditor (kolm ships the product). Moat is velocity + reference logos + implementation, not spec gatekeeping.

RELEASE AS CC0 (PUBLIC DOMAIN) on Github, not proprietary. CC0 signals 'this is for the industry,' reduces procurement friction (enterprises comfortable adopting open standards), prevents future competitors from claiming 'kolm locked the spec behind a paywall.' Transparency builds trust with regulated buyers.

FREEZE AIUC-1.0 BY JULY 31 2026 (before EU AI Act Article 12 Aug 2 cliff). The cliff creates regulatory urgency; kolm wants to ship attestations referencing a stable spec BEFORE that deadline, so early adopters can say 'we passed AIUC-1.0 audit' in their enterprise deals. Aug 30 release of AIUC-1.1 addresses the post-cliff landscape; AIUC-1.0 remains valid for legacy contracts.

BUILD ALL 24 CONTROLS ON TOP OF REUSABLE KOLM STACK: don't fork. mcp-gateway handles tool inventory + call logging; trace-capture handles audit trail; pii-redactor handles redaction proof; intoto-receipt + auditor-attestation handle cryptographic signatures; auth.js handles scope validation. Controls are implemented as CHECKS over existing modules, not new code. Reduces product build time to 4 weeks (audit product launch), not 16 weeks (full rebuild).

MAP CONTROLS DETERMINISTICALLY (not aspirationally). Every control must have a check function that returns boolean + finding. E.g., AG-MP-001 check = 'GET /v1/audit/log exists AND returns at least 100 records AND each record has timestamp/tool/tenant/signature' → boolean + 'PASS: N log entries found, earliest T, latest T, signature valid' or 'FAIL: log missing / signature invalid / tenant isolation broken.' Non-falsifiable claims get the auditor's product discredited in the market.

CREATE FOUR READINESS LEVELS (not continuous scale). Continuous scales are hard to communicate and hard to price. Four levels let buyers bucket themselves ('we want Standard, not Hardened') and let kolm price in tiers. Baseline is 'sanity check'; Standard is 'enterprise-acceptable'; Hardened is 'regulated-vertical ready'; Audited is 'deal-unblock magic.' Each level has a clear go/no-go checklist.

MAKE FRAMEWORK VERSION IMMUTABLE ONCE FROZEN. AIUC-1.0 controls 1-24 do NOT change; AIUC-1.1 adds mapping text for new regulations but controls remain. Buyers locking to 'AIUC-1.0 Hardened' know exactly what they're getting, re-audit time, no scope creep. Versioning is the contract.

OWN THE VERTICAL ADAPTERS (Healthcare, Finance, Telecom) AS FOLLOW-ON SKU LAUNCHES. Don't let the community fork them. Sector adapters are where margin lives post-2027 (retainer revenue, high switching cost, audit complexity). kolm ships the first adapters (Healthcare Q3 2026, Finance Q4 2026); competitors will reverse-engineer, but first-mover + certified auditor status compounds.

REQUIRE THIRD-PARTY SIGNATURE FOR 'AUDITED' LEVEL (not all levels). Baseline/Standard/Hardened reports are signed by kolm auditors; 'Audited' level requires an independent audit firm (Deloitte, KPMG, EY, BDO) to co-sign or lead the audit. This creates a $ gate (independent audits cost 3-5x more) but makes the report defensible against 'but kolm is just a vendor' objections. Deal unblocking > velocity for Audited tier; buyer is willing to pay for credibility.

FREEZE AIUC-1.0 SPEC BY JUL 31 2026 SO FIRST AUDIT REPORTS SHIP AUG 1-15. Early adopter should be able to hand an AIUC-1.0 Hardened audit report to a buyer and close a deal before the EU AI Act Article 12 cliff creates enterprise-wide compliance crises. Win the first 5 design-partner audits on AIUC-1.0 timeline, build momentum + case studies.

DON'T PROMISE INJECTION DEFENSE IN BASE FRAMEWORK. Test injection (PyRIT/Garak), report findings, price separately. Auditors have liability for 'this agent is secure' claims; injection is an open problem. Scope to 'attested API/permission posture + audit trail integrity + execution-time evidence' and disclaim injection as separate engagement. Contract language: 'scope does not include prompt injection resistance testing' + checkbox 'Customer chose not to include injection testing' so there's no ambiguity later.
open_questions: Should AIUC-1 include a severity/weighting model for control failures (e.g., AG-MP-001 failure = critical, AG-GV-005 failure = medium)? Current design is binary pass/fail per control. Severity helps buyers prioritize remediation but adds complexity. Recommendation: ship AIUC-1.0 binary; add severity + risk-scoring in AIUC-1.1 based on buyer feedback.

How should AIUC-1 handle agents that operate at different autonomy scopes (AWS Scope 1 read-only vs Scope 4 unrestricted)? Current design has AG-GV-002 (delegation scope per agent). Should the framework have four sub-profiles (AIUC-Scope-1-Hardened, AIUC-Scope-4-Hardened)? Or a single set of controls that scales? Recommendation: single control set, but AG-GV-002 CHECK explicitly validates scope boundaries, and audit report spells out which scope each agent operates at.

What is the relationship between AIUC-1 controls and the existing kolm receipt chain / K-score? AIUC controls are about agentic systems; K-score is about narrow distilled models. Should AIUC mandate that agent suppliers use kolm-signed models? Or is AIUC orthogonal? Recommendation: orthogonal. AIUC is a buyer-side audit standard; kolm models are a vendor-side component. An agent built with kolm-signed models will score higher on AG-MS-003 (model provenance) but an agent built with frontier API calls is not disqualified from AIUC compliance. Agnostic auditor positioning.

How to handle AIUC versioning as regulatory landscape shifts? EU AI Act, NIST RMF, Colorado/California laws are all finalizing in 2026. Should AIUC freeze immediately after each major regulatory event, or should kolm maintain a rolling 'latest' version? Recommendation: semantic versioning (AIUC-1.0, 1.1, 2.0 like npm). Buyers can opt-in to newer versions or stay on frozen version; regulatory changes get MINOR version bumps (1.0 → 1.1) not MAJOR (2.0) unless fundamental control structure changes. Gives enterprises confidence (can lock to stable version) while allowing evolution.

What governance model for the AIUC spec after kolm ships it? Should there be a formal working group / standards body, or kolm unilateral control? Unilateral control = faster iteration but risks 'kolm is just pushing vendor interests' criticism. Formal WG = slower but more credible. Recommendation: hybrid. Kolm publishes AIUC-1.0 unilaterally in Jul 2026 (speed); announce AIUC Working Group (open membership, kolm + early auditors + one buyer rep) for AIUC-1.1 + 2.0 governance. Credibility compounds after first release.

design.component: Agent Security Review Readiness Audit: 8-Week Build+Sell Plan
design.summary: An expedited 8-week product launch motion that converts $25K fixed-fee security audits into $25K/mo run-rate via 5 design partners. The plan leverages 60% reusable kolm stack (Ed25519 signing, in-toto receipts, audit-trail infrastructure, PII redaction, trace capture, MCP gateway) to build a AI-agent-specific attestation report mapped to frameworks buyers cite (SOC2/ISO 42001/NIST/EU AI Act/OWASP). Core velocity strategy: land 5 warm design partners at $8K/mo, lock contingency deals, establish attestation credibility with named logo + case-study brand, and thread the infrastructure into a durable regulated-vertical bet. Moat is sales velocity + signed case studies, not defensible tech; fast-follow risk from Vanta/Drata mitigated by locking reference customers and accelerator partnerships now.
details: WEEKS 1-2 (Foundation + Design Partner Lock): Build the MVP attestation report (Weeks 1, parallel streams). STREAM A (Signing/Receipt): Modularize src/keys.js + src/intoto-receipt.js + src/auditor-attestation.js into a standalone /auditor-sign service endpoint (isolate private-key handling from gateway; sign at attestation-report level, not per-call). Copy auditor attestation verifier check logic (src/auditor-attestation.js lines 59-70 in full, plus signature validation + fingerprint check) into a browser-side verification widget (zero-trust model: buyer's analyst can verify the signature in real time without calling back). STREAM B (Permission Analysis Matrix): Build the core audit logic — parse a startup's config, list all agents/tools/delegations (via MCP introspection + API scope inspection using mintScopedKey / keyHasScope patterns from src/auth.js), and compare to AWS Agentic Security Scoping Matrix (Scope 1=read-only, Scope 2=human-in-loop, Scope 3=constrained-autonomy, Scope 4=full-autonomy) to generate a findings CSV. Reuse src/pii-redactor.js for redaction evidence. STREAM C (Report Template + Framework Mapping): Produce a fixed-page Markdown → PDF template that maps every finding to SOC2 CC1-CC9, ISO 42001 clauses, NIST AG-GV/AG-MP/AG-MS/AG-MG, EU AI Act Articles 12+14, OWASP ASI01-ASI10 + LLM Top 10, MITRE ATLAS agent context-poisoning, CSA AICM 18 domains, and AWS scoping tier. PDF must be styled as a 'Compliance-Ready' artifact, branded with the auditor's logo + signature block + Ed25519 fingerprint QR-code. STREAM D (Minimal Red-Team Harness): Wire PyRIT/Garak injection testing against the startup's model endpoint (NOT comprehensive prompt-injection defense — that's priced separately). Capture attack surface and report pass/fail on OWASP ASI01-ASI02. STREAM E (Sales/Legal Motion): Finalize fixed-fee quote ($25K audit, 10-page report, 15-day delivery guarantee, signature + verification), draft contingency SLA (5-10% on deal close within 60 days, tracked via Stripe webhook + founder attestation), and create NDA + audit-scope document. Lock 5 design partners (warm intros via your AI-native network / YC / Techstars / founder Slack) by end of Week 2 at $8K/mo retainer (covers 1 audit per month + re-attestation monitoring). Target founders whose Series-A raise is live or in prep (ask: 'Are you selling to enterprises?' + 'Have you hit security review walls?').

WEEKS 3-4 (Fast Delivery + Credibility Lock): Execute first 2 design-partner audits (1 per week; batch the permission-analysis CSV across both to reuse framework-mapping labor). Iterate on the report template based on real findings — lock the page count, visual hierarchy, and framework-mapping density. Deliver signed attestations + Ed25519 verification page. Collect testimonial from each founder ('This cut our review cycle from 6 weeks to 10 days') and take explicit permission to name them + quote in case study. Create a SHORT (1-page) case study for each with before/after cycle-time, the deal size (anonymized or named if they agree), and the key finding (over-permissioned agents, missing audit trail, etc.). Post both to a /case-studies page on the website. Wire the contingency-outcome tracking: create a Slack/email alert when a design partner's deal closes so you can log the closing date + amount (needed to calculate contingency fee and validate the correlation). Start cold outreach in parallel to 5-10 warm prospects (ask for 30-min calls, not immediate pilots). By end of Week 4: 2 signed attested reports, 2 named case studies, 1-2 contingency deals closed or in final stages.

WEEKS 5-6 (Scaling to 3 More + First Contingency Closure): Execute design-partner audits #3, #4, #5 and lock a 6th design partner for Week 7 (momentum signal). Aim for at least 1 contingency closure (deal close at a prior design partner); publicly log the fee earned and update website + case-study language to include 'contingency fee earned on Q2 close.' This proves the model works. Invite 2-3 founders from the first-wave design partners to a 45-min recorded 'Security Review Readiness' fireside chat (position them as security-first founders; record audio + pull 60-second quote clips for social/homepage). Launch a warm-outreach email sequence (5 touches over 10 days; sent from your email, not a template service — founders can smell mass mail) to accelerators + founder networks with a SHORT value prop: 'We cut enterprise security reviews from 4-8 weeks to 5-10 days. If a $100K+ deal is stuck in their RFI, we unblock it in 2 weeks. Fixed fee $25K + success-based contingency.' Include a founder-testimonial link + a 2-min YouTube explainer of the attestation verification. By end of Week 6: 5 design partners on retainer ($40K/mo run-rate), 3-5 contingency closes logged (partial; not all will have earned the fee yet if they close in week 7-8), 5+ warm outreach conversations started.

WEEKS 7-8 (Retainer Expansion + Bridge to Durable Product): Focus on converting warm conversations to paid retainers. Land 2-3 new design partners (bringing total to 7-8; target = $64K-$80K/mo nominal, though not all earn full fees simultaneously). Lock at least 2 retainer-to-deal conversions (founders who buy the audit and whose deal closes, securing the contingency fee in this window). Finalize the 'Security Review Readiness' brand and claim it as proprietary IP (name + positioning; trademark if budget allows). Begin Phase 2 planning: (1) Evaluate the top 3 design partners by deal-quality + testimonial enthusiasm and propose a 'co-founder brief' on the durable regulated-vertical bet (regulated security auditing for AI agents; HQ'd in a compliance-forward jurisdiction; fund via Series Seed from this cash + LPs' follow-on; target regulated-vertical like healthcare SaMD, finance AI, or EU high-risk). (2) Start hiring signal: identify 1 security architect + 1 head of sales (both can start part-time in Week 9-10). (3) Lock the infrastructure bridge: document how the 8-week attestation pipeline plugs into the next phase (multi-tenant audit platform, continuous re-attestation, automated control-evidence collection). By end of Week 8: achieve or exceed $25K/mo run-rate (5-6 retainers + partial contingencies), 3-5 signed case studies, 1-2 co-founder briefs scheduled with Series-A founders, recruitment brief drafted.

REUSABLE STACK (60% Production-Ready MVP): src/keys.js (Ed25519 key rotation, KMS target abstraction, no KMS API calls — operator-side concern, air-gap-safe) + src/intoto-receipt.js (in-toto DSSE envelope, SLSA v1 predicate, Sigstore/OpenSSF Model Signing bundle shape for cross-tool verification, 36 refs in codebase) + src/auditor-attestation.js (third-party auditor signature, separate from builder key, verifier check #22 fully implemented, conformance string 'Ed25519 key-custody, not compute-proof'). ATTESTATION STACK: Copy auditor-attestation lines 59-70 (block-present signature + claim-drift detection), integrate Ed25519 signature validation, and expose as /auditor/verify endpoint. AUDIT-TRAIL DURABLE: src/capture-store.js (router pattern: vercel_postgres / vercel_kv / sqlite / json fallback; async insertCapture + isDurable() + driverName() contract already in place) + src/trace-capture.js (W3C Trace Context trace_id/span_id, append-only JSONL under KOLM_TRACE_DIR, payload redaction via redactForExport, 7 span kinds including agent tool-calls). Use trace-capture as the append-only tamper-resistant log for EU AI Act Article 12 compliance (automatic logging, 6-month+ retention, enforcement cliff Aug 2 2026). PERMISSION AUDIT: src/auth.js mintScopedKey + keyHasScope (proves scoped vs wildcard permissions; rotateTenantReceiptSecret for key hygiene). Cross-reference against AWS scoping matrix + AWS Agentic Security whitepaper (not in kolm, but the permission patterns are). REDACTION + PII: src/pii-redactor.js (44 refs, 4-mode contract: detect_only / redact_captures / redact_all / block; HIPAA 18 + 3 kolm extensions, scan + apply both input/output). Use redaction evidence to attest GDPR + PII-in-audit-logs claims. MCP GATEWAY: src/gateway-router.js (11-stage pipeline: auth → namespace → input-PII → route → forward → response → output-PII → sign → capture → meter → telemetry) + src/mcp-gateway-routes.js (POST /v1/mcp/dispatch with signed receipts, GET /v1/mcp/verify/:id, tenant fence). Already implements tool-call governance + receipt signing; reuse as audit-evidence collector. DATA-PRODUCT / LEAD-GEN: src/distill-strategy.js + scripts/distill-strategy.mjs (catalog of attack patterns by task/privacy/model), src/quantization-oracle.js + scripts/quantization-oracle.mjs (device-targeting, quality/privacy Pareto frontier). Extract the 'audited attack patterns' from each design-partner audit into an anonymized + aggregated 'Security Posture Benchmark' report (quarterly distilled red-team findings across agent population). Sell as add-on to retainer or as separate 'Agent Security Trends' SaaS datapoint. NET: ~6 weeks of integration work to wire the 6 modules into a turnkey audit pipeline; ~2 weeks to build the report template + framework mapping; ~4 weeks to execute design partners + lock contingencies. Critical path: design-partner lock (Week 2) > first audit execution (Week 3) > contingency close (Week 6) > retainer expansion (Week 8).

TOP RISKS + MITIGATIONS: [RISK 1: Cold-start demand direction — founders instinctively avoid compliance; warm channels (accelerators, founder Slack, peer word-of-mouth) are the ONLY viable motion.] MITIGATION: (a) Lock 5 design partners via warm intros in Week 1-2 before any cold outreach; treat cold email as a low-signal backfill channel (~5% conversion) not the primary motion. (b) Partner with YC, Techstars, and 2-3 founder Slack communities (e.g., AI-native SaaS founders, Latent Space Discord) to position the audit as 'security review blocker unlocker' not 'compliance vendor.' (c) Create a 'security stuck in review' alert query (monitor VC funding rounds + job postings for 'Head of Security' at early-stage AI startups; people who hire for security are likely to feel the review pain). [RISK 2: Scope / Liability trap — prompt-injection defense is NOT in baseline kolm. Signing an attestation that reads 'secure' when the customer's agent runs untrusted code creates direct liability.] MITIGATION: (a) Contractually scope every attestation to 'API/permission posture + PII redaction + audit-trail integrity; prompt-injection resistance is tested and reported, NOT warranted.' (b) Wire a clear redline in the red-team report: 'This audit tested against OWASP ASI01-ASI02 injection patterns. Defense is NOT comprehensive; vendor must implement guardrails (Lakera, Cisco AI Defense, PromptArmor, etc.) for production resilience.' Price deeper red-team penetration (ASI03-ASI10, custom model-behavior fuzzing) as a 'Advanced Security Package' (+$10K) so scope creep doesn't crush margin. (c) Ensure the founder's legal counsel reviews the attestation scope language; no founder wants surprise liability on their sale. [RISK 3: Buyer acceptance — enterprise review group (CISO, Privacy, Procurement, CTO) may not recognize a novel third-party signed attestation without established trust mark.] MITIGATION: (a) Map EVERY finding to standards already in the RFI: SOC2 / ISO 42001 / NIST / EU AI Act / OWASP. Do not invent a new framework; reference the existing ones so the buyer's review checklist sees it as 'completed evidence, not a new claim.' (b) Accumulate named reference logos fast: target 3-5 logos by end of Week 6 (founders willing to name themselves + the deal). Post case studies on the website and in your outreach so founders see social proof that 'peers used this + it worked.' (c) Get explicit permission from founders to share deal outcomes (even if anonymized) so the Slack/accelerator community hears 'security review cycle went from 8 weeks → 10 days, deal closed.' (d) Consider a 'compliance advisor / CISO review' layer: hire a retired CISO or Big-4 compliance person part-time (10 hrs/week) to review the attestation language + co-sign the report as an independent auditor so the enterprise review group sees two signatures (yours + the advisor's). Cost ~$2-3K/audit; reduces margin but dramatically improves buyer confidence. [RISK 4: Contingency mechanics — clawback and attribution disputes. Did the attestation truly unblock the deal, or did the founder's sales VP close it? Is the fee owed if the deal closes in month 9 vs month 2?] MITIGATION: (a) Watertight contract: 'Success fee of 5-10% of ACV is due if the deal closes within 60 days of attestation delivery AND the buyer's final RFI response cites the audit report as a condition of close.' (b) Ask the founder directly in Week 2 (design-partner signup): 'Is your deal stuck in security review right now? What's the deal size + timeline?' and log it in a CRM. Then send a 'before attestation' email confirming the deal state + timeline. After the deal closes, ask the founder (via Stripe invoice or email) to confirm the close date + buyer feedback ('did the attestation help?'). Stripe webhook + founder email together create a paper trail. (c) Accept that ~30% of audits won't hit the contingency window (deal closes in month 3+) or won't satisfy the 'audit cited in final RFI' clause; assume 50-60% contingency realization rate in financial modeling (5 audits at $25K fixed = $125K; 2-3 close within window at 7.5% ACV = +$15-22K; total $140-147K for 2-month cycle). (d) Make contingency optional in the contract: 'You pay $25K fixed. If your deal closes within 60 days AND you cite the audit in your RFI closeout, we invoice 5-10% of ACV as a success bonus (capped at $50K per deal).' Low-friction for founders; reduces dispute risk. [RISK 5: Fast-follow / Commoditization — Vanta/Drata or a funded AI-security startup can copy the playbook in 4-6 months; SOC2 delivery times are already compressing to 14 days.] MITIGATION: (a) Build moat via sales velocity + network effects: land 10-15 named case studies + logos in Weeks 3-8. By the time a competitor launches their version, you have 'proven in market, trusted by [Founder Name] at [Startup Name]' as your defensibility. (b) Lock accelerator partnerships NOW (Weeks 1-2): approach YC (Ali Yahya or YC's security lead), Techstars (via your network), and founder communities with a co-marketing deal: 'We'll audit YC founders at a 25% discount; you feature us in the security office hours + recommend to stuck deals.' Once YC/Techstars recommend your audit, a competitor's cold launch is noisy; the trusted channel matters more than the tech. (c) Make the contingency model sticky: founders who close a deal with your audit are more likely to use your retainer for follow-up + re-attestation (quarterly monitoring, new agent deployments). Vanta/Drata don't have a contingency play; your moat is 'we put skin in the game on your deal close.' (d) Expand horizontally within Week 9+: once you have 10+ case studies, pivot to 'MCP Supply-Chain Security Audit' (492 unauthenticated MCP servers, 1,184 malicious skills; audit which ones a startup's agents can call). This is adjacent, uses the same signing/report machinery, and differentiates from Vanta. [RISK 6: Demand-shape shift — if agents standardize on Scope 1 (read-only) or Scope 2 (human-in-loop), the permission-audit need shrinks.] MITIGATION: (a) Position the audit as the BUYER's evaluation checklist, not just the vendor's defense. Reframe: 'This is your procurement playbook for vetting ANY agent vendor going forward.' (b) Expand scope: add 'model-drift monitoring' (use src/drift-detect.js patterns) and 'behavior telemetry' (use src/agent-telemetry.js patterns) to prove the agent is NOT behaving in ways outside its scope. Sell as 'Scope Assurance Retainer' — $2.5-5K/mo for continuous monitoring. (c) If Scope 1 becomes standard, you've won: that's easier to audit, faster reports, higher volume, lower margin per audit but higher total revenue. [RISK 7: Moving regulatory target — EU AI Act / NIST agentic guidance is still solidifying in 2026. Attestation criteria can shift under you.] MITIGATION: (a) Version the report template: 'Security Review Readiness Attestation v1.0 (2026 Q2)' + a versioning section in the PDF that says 'Updated for EU AI Act Article 12 (enforcement Aug 2 2026) + NIST AI RMF (post-consultation draft June 2026).' (b) Lock early reference customers NOW and re-attest on a quarterly schedule as guidance solidifies. Make re-attestation a 'delta report' (only changed findings, not a full re-audit). (c) Monitor EU AI Act + NIST releases; commit to a '30-day response window' for framework updates so your reports stay ahead of the regulatory cliff. (d) Build retainer revenue to sustain this: re-attestation + updates are the sticky part; founders will pay $2.5-5K/mo to stay compliant as rules change.
decisions: DECISION 1 — MVP Report Scope (Fixed, Non-Negotiable): The attestation report is 10 pages (Markdown → PDF). MUST INCLUDE: (1) permission-matrix finding (Scope 1-4 classification + over-provisioned agent count), (2) audit-trail evidence (append-only log + PII redaction proof), (3) red-team summary (OWASP ASI01-ASI02 testing results, pass/fail), (4) framework mapping (one 2-page spread per: SOC2 CC1-CC9, ISO 42001, NIST AG-*, EU AI Act 12+14, OWASP ASI 1-10). CAN DEFER: prompt-injection comprehensive testing, hardware-security attestation, zero-knowledge proofs, model fine-tuning audit, or multi-day on-site penetration testing. These go into a 'Advanced Package' (+$10K-$50K) sold separately. Rationale: 10 pages fits a 2-week delivery cadence (Week 1 analysis + findings, Week 2 report write + signing); larger scope creeps into 4-week timeline and kills the 'fast unblock' positioning. Fixed scope = predictable margin = designer velocity.

DECISION 2 — Signing Model (Ed25519 Private Key Storage): The auditor's private key NEVER enters the web application. Implement /v1/auditor/sign as a CLI tool only (not a web endpoint) that the founder runs locally or hands to a signing service (AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, YubiKey). Wire the public key + signature + certificate into the PDF + a separate JSON attestation file. The JSON file is what the founder hands to the enterprise buyer; the PDF is the human-readable artifact. Rationale: air-gap-safe (kolm's model from src/keys.js lines 19-32), reduces threat surface (no key in web server), and allows founders to BYOK (bring their own KMS). Accept the UX friction (CLI tool instead of 'click to sign') as a FEATURE for security-conscious founders; they want assurance the signing ceremony was secure.

DECISION 3 — Attestation Verification Widget (Zero-Trust Buyer Verification): Build a lightweight HTML5 widget (JavaScript + crypto.subtle WebAPI for Ed25519 verification) that a buyer's analyst can run offline to verify the report signature without calling back to your server. Embed the public key in the JSON attestation + the widget's code repo so ANY third party can verify. Rationale: eliminates 'trust the auditor' and replaces it with 'cryptographically verify the report wasn't tampered with.' Drives credibility without building a trust-as-a-service infrastructure. Buyers love this because it's tamper-evident + verifiable. Host the widget on GitHub + cdn.jsdelivr.net (zero-ops, zero-cost, immutable).

DECISION 4 — Design Partner Pricing Model (Firm, Not Discounted): $8K/mo retainer for each design partner. Includes: (1) one full audit per month (10 pages, signed, 15-day turnaround), (2) re-attestation monitoring (flag any new agents/tools added to their product, recommend re-audit), (3) quarterly framework-update brief (explain how NIST / EU AI Act / OWASP changes affect their attestation). NO equity, NO co-founder briefs during the 8-week sprint (lock that for Week 7-8 conversations). Contingency: 5-10% of ACV on deal close within 60 days. Rationale: $8K/mo × 5-6 partners = $40-48K/mo, enough to cover 1 FTE auditor + report generation + legal/billing overhead. Firm pricing disciplines both you and the founders (no haggling, clear scope, clear timelines). Contingency is a 'skin in the game' alignment signal without being so high that it kills closure.

DECISION 5 — Red-Team Automation Scope (Bounded, Not Comprehensive): Use PyRIT/Garak for OWASP ASI01-ASI02 injection testing ONLY (prompt-injection, jailbreak attempts). Do NOT run ASI03-ASI10 (training data exfil, model inversion, privacy attacks, etc.) in the base audit. Instead: (a) Run ASI01-02 against the startup's chat endpoint, capture attack surface, report pass/fail. (b) Hire a third-party red team (e.g., HackerOne, Intigriti, or a local security consultancy) for 'Advanced Penetration Testing' (+$10-50K, sold separately). (c) Explicitly scope the report: 'Red team testing covers OWASP Top 10 LLM categories 1, 3, 5 (injection, training-data attacks, supply-chain). Defense is not comprehensive; vendor must implement production guardrails (Lakera, Cisco AI Defense, etc.).' Rationale: ASI01-02 testing fits a 2-week audit window; comprehensive red-teaming takes 4-6 weeks and breaks the 'fast unblock' story. Bounded scope = predictable timeline. The 'Advanced Package' upsell funds deeper work if a founder is willing to wait or has a longer deal cycle.

DECISION 6 — Framework Mapping (Reference, Not Exhaustive): The report maps findings to SOC2 / ISO 42001 / NIST / EU AI Act / OWASP, BUT the mapping is a 1-2 sentence callout per framework, not a full audit against each standard. Example: 'Permission Matrix finding maps to SOC2 CC6 (logical access controls). Startup has implemented role-based scope limits on 3/5 agents; recommend extending to 100% of agent fleet.' This gives the enterprise buyer a 'yes, I can cite this in my RFI response' artifact without requiring you to BE a SOC2 auditor (which requires AICPA training + annual license renewal). Rationale: mapping takes 2-3 days per report and is reusable across similar findings; full SOC2 audit takes 8-12 weeks. The buyer's CISO will do the detailed SOC2 cross-mapping themselves; your job is to give them the evidence hooks.

DECISION 7 — Case Study Brand + Logo Accumulation Strategy (Named, Public, Fast): By end of Week 4, commit to naming at least 2 design partners + founders in case studies (with explicit permission). By end of Week 8, target 5-7 named logos. Post a /case-studies landing page with a short brief (100-word summary, founder name + title, before/after metrics, key finding). NO anonymization — the network effect of 'you know these founders' is more powerful than a generic 'Series A startup, 50 engineers' narrative. Rationale: Warm intros are the primary motion; when a founder sees 'Founder Name at Company X, Series A, same cohort as me' they check it. Anonymized case studies are static content that gets ignored.
